options: Async iteration: await foreach (var item in source)
intermediate.explanation: IAsyncEnumerable<T> supports async streaming with await foreach.

intermediate.question: What is ConfigureAwait?
intermediate.explanation: ConfigureAwait(false) avoids capturing SynchronizationContext; improves perf.

intermediate.question: What is ValueTuple?
intermediate.explanation: ValueTuple is mutable struct; supports deconstruction and named elements.

intermediate.question: What is Index/Range?
options: ^ (from end) and .. (range): arr[^1] is last; arr[1..3] is slice
intermediate.explanation: ^1 last element; arr[..] entire array; arr[1..^1] without first/last.

advanced.question: What is reflection?
options: Runtime type inspection and dynamic invocation
advanced.explanation: Type t = obj.GetType(); t.GetMethod("DoWork").Invoke(obj, args);

advanced.question: What is Assembly?
advanced.explanation: Assembly.GetExecutingAssembly(); assembly.GetTypes() enumerates types.

advanced.question: What is System.Reflection.Emit?
advanced.explanation: AssemblyBuilder, TypeBuilder, ILGenerator emit IL instructions dynamically.

advanced.question: What is Expression<T>?
options: Expression tree representing lambda as data
advanced.explanation: Expression<Func<int, int>> expr = x => x * x; expr.Compile() makes delegate.

advanced.question: What are attributes?
advanced.explanation: [AttributeUsage(AttributeTargets.Class)] class MyAttr : Attribute { }

advanced.question: What is Conditional attribute?
options: [Conditional("DEBUG")] method called only in DEBUG builds
advanced.explanation: Conditional methods are removed from release builds by compiler.

advanced.question: What is CallerMemberName?
options: Compiler provides caller method name as string
advanced.explanation: [CallerMemberName] string member = "" gets calling method name automatically.

advanced.question: What is dynamic?
advanced.explanation: dynamic d = GetResult(); d.Calculate() resolved at runtime via DLR.

advanced.question: What is the DLR?
options: Dynamic Language Runtime for dynamic dispatch
advanced.explanation: DLR supports dynamic C#, IronPython, IronRuby bindings.

advanced.question: What are extension methods?
options: Add methods to existing types: static void Foo(this string s)
advanced.explanation: Extension: public static int WordCount(this string str) => str.Split().Length;

advanced.question: What are partial classes?
options: Splits class across files: partial class MyClass { }
advanced.explanation: Partial classes combine multiple files into single type at compile time.

advanced.question: What is unsafe code?
advanced.explanation: unsafe { int* p = &x; *p = 5; } requires /unsafe compiler flag.

advanced.question: What are fixed pointers?
options: Pins object so GC doesn't move it: fixed (int* p = &arr[0])
advanced.explanation: fixed statement prevents garbage collector from relocating memory.

advanced.question: What is stackalloc?
options: Allocates memory on stack (fast, no GC): Span<int> s = stackalloc int[10]
advanced.explanation: stackalloc in Span<T> context: no unsafe keyword needed.

advanced.question: What is Interlocked?
advanced.explanation: Interlocked provides atomic CompareExchange, Add, Increment, Exchange.

advanced.question: What is volatile?
options: volatile int flag; prevents compiler/CPU reordering
advanced.explanation: volatile ensures reads/writes are not cached; visible across threads.

advanced.question: What is ThreadPool?
options: Manages worker threads for parallel work
advanced.explanation: ThreadPool.QueueUserWorkItem(state => DoWork()) runs on pooled thread.

advanced.question: What is TPL (Task Parallel Library)?
advanced.explanation: Task.Run(() => Compute()); Parallel.For(0, 100, i => Process(i));

advanced.question: What is Parallel.For/ForEach?
options: Parallel loops: Parallel.For(0, n, i => Compute(i))
advanced.explanation: Parallel.ForEach(collection, item => Process(item)) partitions work.

advanced.question: What is Lazy<T>?
options: Lazy initialization: Lazy<Expensive> exp = new Lazy<Expensive>()
advanced.explanation: Lazy<T> is thread-safe; exp.Value triggers construction on first access.

advanced.question: What is LazyInitializer?
advanced.explanation: LazyInitializer.EnsureInitialized is lock-free, more performant than Lazy.

advanced.question: What is ThreadLocal<T>?
options: Thread-local storage: ThreadLocal<int> tls = new ThreadLocal<int>()
advanced.explanation: ThreadLocal<T> each thread sees own value; disposed at end.

advanced.question: What is AsyncLocal<T>?
options: Async context local: flows with execution context
advanced.explanation: AsyncLocal<T> flows across async/await boundaries automatically.

advanced.question: What is ExecutionContext?
options: Captures ambient context (security, locale, async local)
advanced.explanation: ExecutionContext flows across async points; captured in async methods.

advanced.question: What is SynchronizationContext?
advanced.explanation: SynchronizationContext.Post sends work to UI thread in WPF/WinForms.

advanced.question: What is IProgress<T>?
options: Reports progress: IProgress<double> progress = new Progress<double>()
advanced.explanation: progress.Report(0.5) invokes Progress<T> handler on captured context.

advanced.question: What is IObserver<T>/IObservable<T>?
options: Reactive pattern: observer subscribes to observable
advanced.explanation: Subscribe: observable.Subscribe(observer); OnNext/OnError/OnCompleted.

advanced.question: What is System.Reactive?
options: Reactive Extensions for LINQ to events/async
advanced.explanation: Rx: Observable.FromEventPattern, .Throttle, .CombineLatest operators.

advanced.question: What is TPL Dataflow?
options: Actor-like data pipeline: BufferBlock, ActionBlock, TransformBlock
advanced.explanation: Dataflow: var block = new ActionBlock<int>(x => Process(x)); block.Post(42);

advanced.question: What is System.IO.Pipelines?
advanced.explanation: Pipelines reduce allocations in I/O-heavy scenarios (Kestrel).

advanced.question: What is System.Text.Json?
advanced.explanation: JsonSerializer.Serialize(obj); JsonSerializer.Deserialize<T>(json);

advanced.question: What is Source Generators?
advanced.explanation: [Generator] class MyGenerator : ISourceGenerator generates files at compile.

advanced.question: What is IncrementalGenerator?
advanced.explanation: IncrementalGenerator caches previous outputs for faster rebuilds.

advanced.question: What is AOT compilation?
options: Ahead-of-Time: compiles IL to native before runtime
advanced.explanation: Native AOT: no JIT, no IL, smaller binaries, faster startup.

advanced.question: What is ReadyToRun?
options: Pre-compiled native code shipped with assembly
advanced.explanation: R2R images reduce JIT time; cross-gen generates during publish.

advanced.question: What is P/Invoke?
options: Calls native functions: [DllImport("user32.dll")] static extern int MessageBox()
advanced.explanation: P/Invoke marshals parameters; DllImport specifies DLL and calling convention.

advanced.question: What is COM interop?
options: Interacts with COM components via RCW
advanced.explanation: COM interop creates Runtime Callable Wrapper (RCW) for COM objects.

advanced.question: What is WinRT interop?
advanced.explanation: WinRT interop uses projected types; CsWinRT generates C# bindings.

advanced.question: What is MemoryMappedFile?
options: Maps file to virtual memory for fast access
advanced.explanation: MemoryMappedFile.CreateFromFile("data.bin") creates view accessor for read/write.

advanced.question: What is SafeHandle?
options: Wrapper for native handles with finalization
advanced.explanation: SafeHandle ensures proper cleanup; CriticalHandle for constrained execution.

advanced.question: What is constrained execution?
options: CER (Constrained Execution Region): protected against async exceptions
advanced.explanation: RuntimeHelpers.PrepareConstrainedRegions() prepares code that must run.

advanced.question: What is GC.AddMemoryPressure?
advanced.explanation: AddMemoryPressure(n_bytes) helps GC schedule collection appropriately.

advanced.question: What is GCNotification?
advanced.explanation: GC.RegisterForFullGCNotification(); GC.WaitForFullGCApproach() callback.

advanced.question: What is Span<T> vs Memory<T> vs ReadOnlySequence<T>?
options: Span: stack; Memory: heap; ReadOnlySequence: disjoint segments
advanced.explanation: Span<T> for sync; Memory<T> for async; ReadOnlySequence for pipe data.

advanced.question: What is System.Numerics?
advanced.explanation: Vector.IsHardwareAccelerated checks CPU; Vector<byte>.Count = 16/32.

advanced.question: What is System.Runtime.Intrinsics?
options: CPU-specific intrinsics: Avx2, Sse2, Aes instructions
advanced.explanation: Avx2.Add(left, right) maps to VADDSD instruction; requires hardware support.

advanced.question: What is data parallelism?
advanced.explanation: Parallel.For processes data in chunks; PLINQ processes partitions.

advanced.question: What is task parallelism?
advanced.explanation: Task.Run(() => Work1()); Task.Run(() => Work2()) runs tasks concurrently.

advanced.question: What is SharedMemory (System.Buffer)?
advanced.explanation: Buffer.MemoryCopy avoids bounds checking; for performance-critical scenarios.

advanced.question: What is reflection?
options: Runtime type inspection and dynamic invocation
advanced.explanation: Type t = obj.GetType(); t.GetMethod("DoWork").Invoke(obj, args);

beginner.question: What is Go?
options: Statically typed, compiled language by Google
beginner.explanation: Go is known for simplicity, fast compilation, and built-in concurrency.

beginner.question: What is a package?
beginner.explanation: package main defines an executable; other names are reusable packages.

beginner.question: What is import?
beginner.explanation: import ("fmt"; "os") imports standard library packages.

beginner.question: What is func?
options: Function declaration: func add(x int, y int) int { }
beginner.explanation: Functions can return multiple values: func div(a, b int) (int, error)

beginner.question: What is a variable declaration?
options: var x int = 5 or x := 5 (type inference)
beginner.explanation: := is short declaration; var for explicit type or zero values.

beginner.question: What are basic types?
options: int, float64, string, bool, byte, rune
beginner.explanation: int is platform-dependent; int8, int16, int32, int64 for specific sizes.

beginner.question: What is zero value?
options: Default value: 0 for numbers, "" for strings, false for bool, nil for pointers
beginner.explanation: Variables without explicit initializer get zero values.

beginner.question: What is a pointer?
beginner.explanation: p holds address; *p dereferences; &v takes address.

beginner.question: What is a struct?
options: Composite type: type Person struct { Name string; Age int }
beginner.explanation: Structs group fields; accessed with dot: person.Name.

beginner.question: What is an interface?
options: Method set contract: type Writer interface { Write([]byte) (int, error) }
beginner.explanation: Interfaces are satisfied implicitly; no implements keyword needed.

beginner.question: What is a method?
options: Function with receiver: func (p Person) Greet() string
beginner.explanation: Value receiver (p Person) receives copy; pointer receiver (p *Person) can mutate.

beginner.question: What is a slice?
beginner.explanation: s := make([]int, 0, 5); append(s, 1) grows slice beyond capacity.

beginner.question: What is a map?
beginner.explanation: v, ok := m["key"] checks existence; delete(m, "key") removes.

beginner.question: What is a range loop?
options: Iterates over slices/maps/strings/channels: for i, v := range slice
beginner.explanation: for k, v := range map { ... }; for i, c := range "hello" { ... }

beginner.question: What is defer?
options: Defers function execution until surrounding function returns
beginner.explanation: defer file.Close() ensures cleanup; executed LIFO order.

beginner.question: What is panic?
options: Runtime error that unwinds stack (like exception)
beginner.explanation: panic("something bad") unless recovered with recover().

beginner.question: What is recover?
options: Regains control after panic in deferred function
beginner.explanation: defer func() { if r := recover(); r != nil { log.Println(r) } }()

beginner.question: What is error interface?
options: Built-in: type error interface { Error() string }
beginner.explanation: Functions return error as last value: if err != nil { return err }

beginner.question: What is a goroutine?
beginner.explanation: Goroutines are multiplexed onto OS threads by Go runtime.

beginner.question: What is a channel?
options: Typed conduit for goroutine communication: ch := make(chan int)
beginner.explanation: ch <- value sends; <-ch receives; close(ch) closes.

beginner.question: What is a buffered channel?
beginner.explanation: Sends block only when buffer full; receives block when empty.

beginner.question: What is select?
beginner.explanation: select { case v := <-ch1: ... case <-ch2: ... default: ... }

beginner.question: What is sync.Mutex?
beginner.explanation: mu.Lock(); ...; mu.Unlock() protects shared resources.

beginner.question: What is sync.WaitGroup?
options: Waits for goroutine collection to finish
beginner.explanation: wg.Add(1); go fn(); wg.Wait() blocks until wg.Done() called.

beginner.question: What is atomic?
beginner.explanation: sync/atomic package provides lock-free synchronization.

beginner.question: What is context?
options: Carries deadlines, cancellation, and request-scoped values
beginner.explanation: context.WithTimeout(parent, duration) creates cancelable context.

beginner.question: What is a string?
beginner.explanation: Strings are read-only; len(s) gives bytes, not runes.

beginner.question: What is a rune?
options: Unicode code point: alias for int32
beginner.explanation: rune represents a Unicode character; use range to iterate runes.

beginner.question: What is a byte?
beginner.explanation: []byte("hello") converts string to byte slice.

beginner.question: What is make vs new?
options: make: slices/maps/channels; new: returns pointer to zero value
beginner.explanation: make allocates and initializes; new returns *T with zero value.

beginner.question: What is nil?
options: Zero value for pointers, slices, maps, channels, interfaces, functions
beginner.explanation: nil slice has length 0; nil map cannot be written to.

beginner.question: What is go fmt?
options: Formats Go source code to standard style
beginner.explanation: gofmt (or go fmt) automatically formats code with tabs.

beginner.question: What is go mod?
beginner.explanation: go mod init, go mod tidy, go mod vendor manage dependencies.

beginner.question: What is go build?
beginner.explanation: go build compiles; go install places binary in $GOPATH/bin.

beginner.question: What is go test?
beginner.explanation: go test ./... runs all tests; _test.go files contain test functions.

beginner.question: What is a test function?
beginner.explanation: t.Error(), t.Fatal(), t.Log() provide test output and control.

beginner.question: What is a table-driven test?
options: Tests with struct of cases: tests := []struct{ ... }
beginner.explanation: for _, tt := range tests { t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) { ... }) }

beginner.question: What is go vet?
beginner.explanation: go vet detects issues like unreachable code, suspicious locks.

beginner.question: What is a build tag?
beginner.explanation: Build tags include/exclude files based on OS, arch, or custom tags.

beginner.question: What is cgo?
beginner.explanation: import "C" allows calling C functions with special comment directives.

beginner.question: What is JSON handling?
beginner.explanation: type User struct { Name string `json:"name"` }; json.Marshal(user)

beginner.question: What is struct tags?
beginner.explanation: Tags are used by encoders, validators, and ORMs for config.

beginner.question: What is a blank identifier (_)?
beginner.explanation: _ discards returned values or imports packages solely for side effects.

beginner.question: What is init function?
options: Runs on package initialization, before main()
beginner.explanation: init() runs in declaration order; useful for setting up globals.

beginner.question: What is a closure?
beginner.explanation: adder := func(x int) func(int) int { return func(y int) int { return x + y } }

beginner.question: What is generics (Go 1.18+)?
options: Type parameters: func Fn[T any](x T) T { return x }
beginner.explanation: func Map[T, U any](s []T, f func(T) U) []U { ... }

beginner.question: What is a type constraint?
beginner.explanation: constraints.Ordered includes integers and floats for sorting.

beginner.question: What is type inference with generics?
options: Go often infers type params: result := Map(slice, fn)
beginner.explanation: Go infers from arguments most of the time; specify when needed.

beginner.question: What is the any constraint?
options: Alias for interface{}: accepts any type
beginner.explanation: any accepts all types; use more specific constraints when possible.

beginner.question: What is comparable constraint?
beginner.explanation: comparable enables functions that compare values with ==.

intermediate.question: What are goroutines?
intermediate.explanation: Goroutines are multiplexed onto OS threads; stack starts at ~4KB.

intermediate.question: What is a channel?
intermediate.explanation: ch <- 5 sends; <-ch receives; channels are typed and thread-safe.

intermediate.question: What is a buffered channel?
intermediate.explanation: Buffered channel blocks send only when full; receive when empty.

intermediate.question: What is select?
intermediate.explanation: select { case msg := <-ch1: ... case <-time.After(1s): ... }

intermediate.question: What is a mutex?
intermediate.explanation: var mu sync.Mutex; mu.Lock(); defer mu.Unlock(); protects shared state.

intermediate.question: What is RWMutex?
intermediate.explanation: RLock() for reads; Lock() for writes; better than Mutex for read-heavy.

intermediate.question: What is sync.WaitGroup?
intermediate.explanation: wg.Add(1); go func() { defer wg.Done(); ... }; wg.Wait()

intermediate.question: What is sync.Once?
intermediate.explanation: once.Do(func() { init() }) is safe for concurrent callers.

intermediate.question: What is sync.Cond?
intermediate.explanation: cond.Wait() releases lock; cond.Signal() wakes one; cond.Broadcast() all.

intermediate.question: What is sync.Pool?
options: Temporary object pool to reduce GC pressure
intermediate.explanation: pool.Get() returns interface{}; pool.Put(x) reuses allocated objects.

intermediate.question: What is atomic?
intermediate.explanation: atomic.AddInt64(&counter, 1); atomic.LoadPointer(&ptr); atomic.StoreInt32(&val, 5)

intermediate.question: What is context?
intermediate.explanation: ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(parent, 5s); defer cancel(); select { case <-ctx.Done(): ... }

intermediate.question: What is context.WithValue?
intermediate.explanation: ctx = context.WithValue(ctx, key, val); val = ctx.Value(key) (untyped).

intermediate.question: What is context cancellation propagation?
options: Parent cancel cancels all derived contexts
intermediate.explanation: When parent ctx is cancelled, all children with derived contexts cancel.

intermediate.question: What is error handling in Go?
options: Functions return error as last value
intermediate.explanation: if err != nil { return err } is the Go idiom.

intermediate.question: What is error wrapping?
intermediate.explanation: errors.Is(err, target) checks; errors.As(err, &target) unwraps.

intermediate.question: What is errors.Is?
options: Checks if error matches target (unwraps chain)
intermediate.explanation: errors.Is(err, io.EOF) returns true if any wrapped error is EOF.

intermediate.question: What is errors.As?
intermediate.explanation: var target *MyError; errors.As(err, &target) fills target if matches.

intermediate.question: What is panic/recover?
options: Panic: unexpected error; recover: catch in deferred fn
intermediate.explanation: defer func() { if r := recover(); r != nil { log.Println(r) } }()

intermediate.question: What is defer?
options: Defers function execution until surrounding returns
intermediate.explanation: defer file.Close() runs at function exit; LIFO order for multiple defers.

intermediate.question: What is testing in Go?
options: go test runs *_test.go files with TestXxx(t *testing.T)
intermediate.explanation: t *testing.T for errors; t.Error(), t.Fatal(), t.Log() reporting methods.

intermediate.question: What is table-driven test?
options: Test cases in slice: tests := []struct{ name string; input int; want int }
intermediate.explanation: Table-driven: for _, tt := range tests { t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) { ... }) }

intermediate.question: What is go test -bench?
intermediate.explanation: b.ResetTimer(); for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ { fn() } measures performance.

intermediate.question: What is fuzzing?
options: go test -fuzz: random inputs to find bugs
intermediate.explanation: func FuzzXxx(f *testing.F) { f.Add("seed"); f.Fuzz(func(t *testing.T, s string) { ... }) }

intermediate.question: What is go vet?
intermediate.explanation: go vet reports potential bugs: unreachable code, lock misuse, etc.

intermediate.question: What is gofmt?
options: Formats Go code to standard style
intermediate.explanation: gofmt -w file.go fixes formatting; gofmt -d shows diffs.

intermediate.question: What is golangci-lint?
options: Aggregated linter runner (many linters in one)
intermediate.explanation: golangci-lint runs govet, staticcheck, errcheck, gosimple, unused, etc.

intermediate.question: What is staticcheck?
options: Advanced Go static analysis (SAxxxx, STxxxx checks)
intermediate.explanation: staticcheck detects unused code, incorrect usage, style issues.

intermediate.question: What is go mod?
options: Go module system: go mod init, tidy, vendor, verify
intermediate.explanation: go mod init example.com/myapp; go mod tidy cleans dependencies.

intermediate.question: What is go workspace?
options: Go 1.18+: go.work file for multi-module development
intermediate.explanation: go work init ./mod1 ./mod2; go work use ./local_mod for local dev.

intermediate.question: What is go generate?
intermediate.explanation: //go:generate stringer -type=Pill generates String() method.

intermediate.question: What is go embed?
intermediate.explanation: //go:embed static/index.html var content embed.FS embeds at compile time.

intermediate.question: What is net/http?
intermediate.explanation: http.HandleFunc("/", handler); http.ListenAndServe(":8080", nil)

intermediate.question: What is http.ServerMux?
intermediate.explanation: mux := http.NewServeMux(); mux.HandleFunc("/api", apiHandler)

intermediate.question: What is http.Handler?
intermediate.explanation: type Handler interface { ServeHTTP(http.ResponseWriter, *http.Request) }

intermediate.question: What is middleware pattern?
options: Handler wrapping: func logging(next http.Handler) http.Handler
intermediate.explanation: return http.HandlerFunc(func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) { log.Println(r.URL); next.ServeHTTP(w, r) })

intermediate.question: What is encoding/json?
intermediate.explanation: json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&v) streams JSON from reader.

intermediate.question: What is struct tags?
intermediate.explanation: Tags are reflect-accessible: field.Tag.Get("json") retrieves tag value.

intermediate.question: What is database/sql?
options: SQL database interface: sql.DB, sql.Rows, sql.Row
intermediate.explanation: db.Query("SELECT * FROM users"); for rows.Next() { rows.Scan(&id, &name) }

intermediate.question: What is GORM?
intermediate.explanation: GORM: db.Model(&user).Update("name", "new"); db.Preload("Orders").Find(&users)

intermediate.question: What is SQLx?
intermediate.explanation: sqlx: rows.StructScan(&users) maps rows directly to struct slices.

intermediate.question: What is log/slog?
intermediate.explanation: slog.Info("user logged in", "user_id", id, "ip", r.RemoteAddr)

intermediate.question: What is testing/slogtest?
intermediate.explanation: slogtest.TestHandler(h, results) verifies handler produces correct records.

intermediate.question: What is slices package?
options: Go 1.21: generics slices functions: slices.Sort, slices.Index
intermediate.explanation: slices.Sort(s); slices.Index(s, v); slices.Delete(s, i, j); slices.Compact(s)

intermediate.question: What is maps package?
intermediate.explanation: maps.Keys(m) returns slice of keys; maps.Values(m) returns values.

intermediate.question: What is cmp package?
options: Go 1.21: cmp.Compare, cmp.Less for ordered types
intermediate.explanation: cmp.Compare(5, 3) returns 1; cmp.Less("a", "b") returns true.

intermediate.question: What is reflect?
intermediate.explanation: t := reflect.TypeOf(obj); v := reflect.ValueOf(obj); v.Kind() == reflect.Struct.

intermediate.question: What is unsafe.Pointer?
intermediate.explanation: unsafe.Pointer(&x) converts any pointer type; used with uintptr for arithmetic.

intermediate.question: What is uintptr?
options: Integer large enough for pointer arithmetic
intermediate.explanation: uintptr(p) + offset computes address; GC may move object during computation.

intermediate.question: What is go:build tags?
intermediate.explanation: //go:build !windows excludes file on Windows; _linux.go compiles on Linux.

advanced.question: What is runtime.Gosched?
advanced.explanation: Gosched() yields current goroutine; scheduler runs another goroutine.

advanced.question: What is runtime.GOMAXPROCS?
options: Sets maximum OS threads for goroutines
advanced.explanation: runtime.GOMAXPROCS(0) returns current setting; default = CPU cores.

advanced.question: What is runtime.NumGoroutine?
advanced.explanation: Useful for detecting goroutine leaks: before/after comparison.

advanced.question: What is runtime.GC?
advanced.explanation: runtime.GC() blocks until GC completes; debug.FreeOSMemory() releases to OS.

advanced.question: What is runtime.ReadMemStats?
options: Reads memory statistics into MemStats struct
advanced.explanation: var m runtime.MemStats; runtime.ReadMemStats(&m); fmt.Println(m.Alloc)

advanced.question: What is pprof?
advanced.explanation: go tool pprof http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/heap analyzes memory.

advanced.question: What is trace?
advanced.explanation: trace.Start(f); defer trace.Stop(); go tool trace trace.out visualizes.

advanced.question: What is GODEBUG?
options: Runtime debug environment variables: gctrace=1, schedtrace=1
advanced.explanation: GODEBUG=gctrace=1 prints GC timings; schedtrace=1000 prints scheduler.

advanced.question: What is escape analysis?
options: Compiler determines if value escapes to heap
advanced.explanation: Values that escape to heap cause GC pressure; local values on stack.

advanced.question: What is inlining?
options: Function body expanded at callsite (reduces overhead)
advanced.explanation: Go inlines small functions; -gcflags="-m" shows inlining decisions.

advanced.question: What is bounds check elimination?
options: Compiler removes redundant array bounds checks
advanced.explanation: Compiler eliminates bounds checks in obvious safe loops for performance.

advanced.question: What is cgo?
options: Calls C code from Go: import "C" with cgo pragmas
advanced.explanation: // #include <stdio.h> import "C"; C.puts(C.CString("hello")) calls C.

advanced.question: What is cgo overhead?
options: C call costs: stack switch, CGo costs ~50-100ns
advanced.explanation: Cgo is expensive; batch C calls or minimize crossing boundary.

advanced.question: What is syscall?
options: Raw OS system calls: syscall.Read, syscall.Write
advanced.explanation: syscall.ForkExec("/bin/ls", args, env) creates OS process.

advanced.question: What is exec?
advanced.explanation: cmd := exec.Command("git", "status"); out, err := cmd.Output()

advanced.question: What is os/exec with context?
advanced.explanation: Cancel ctx kills process; cmd.Wait() for exit status.

advanced.question: What is net/http/httptest?
options: Testing HTTP servers/clients without real network
advanced.explanation: ts := httptest.NewServer(handler); defer ts.Close(); resp, _ := http.Get(ts.URL)

advanced.question: What is httptest.ResponseRecorder?
options: Records HTTP response for testing handlers
advanced.explanation: rr := httptest.NewRecorder(); handler.ServeHTTP(rr, req); rr.Code == 200

advanced.question: What is io.Reader/Writer?
options: Stream interfaces: Read(p []byte) (n int, err error)
advanced.explanation: io.Copy(w, r) copies from reader to writer; used for streaming.

advanced.question: What is io.Pipe?
advanced.explanation: pr, pw := io.Pipe(); go func() { json.NewEncoder(pw).Encode(v); pw.Close() }()

advanced.question: What is io.MultiReader?
advanced.explanation: r := io.MultiReader(r1, r2); reads all from r1, then r2.

advanced.question: What is io.MultiWriter?
advanced.explanation: w := io.MultiWriter(f, os.Stdout); fmt.Fprint(w, "log") writes to both.

advanced.question: What is io.TeeReader?
options: Reads and writes to a writer simultaneously
advanced.explanation: tr := io.TeeReader(r, w); read from tr also writes to w (like tee).

advanced.question: What is bufio?
advanced.explanation: scanner := bufio.NewScanner(r); for scanner.Scan() { line := scanner.Text() }

advanced.question: What is bufio.Scanner?
advanced.explanation: Scanner splits by default on newlines; split func configurable (ScanWords).

advanced.question: What is compress/gzip?
advanced.explanation: gw := gzip.NewWriter(w); gw.Write(data); gw.Close() compresses data.

advanced.question: What is archive/tar?
advanced.explanation: tr := tar.NewReader(r); for { hdr, err := tr.Next(); ... } reads tar.

advanced.question: What is encoding/gob?
advanced.explanation: enc := gob.NewEncoder(w); enc.Encode(v) for efficient Go-specific encoding.

advanced.question: What is encoding/binary?
advanced.explanation: binary.Read(r, binary.LittleEndian, &data) reads binary struct.

advanced.question: What is protobuf?
options: Protocol Buffers: protoc-gen-go for code generation
advanced.explanation: proto.Marshal(&msg); proto.Unmarshal(data, &msg) binary efficient format.

advanced.question: What is gRPC?
advanced.explanation: grpc.Dial("server:50051"); client := pb.NewServiceClient(conn); resp, _ := client.Call(ctx, req)

advanced.question: What is gRPC streaming?
options: Server, client, or bidirectional streaming RPC
advanced.explanation: stream, _ := client.ServerStreaming(ctx, req); for { msg, err := stream.Recv() }

advanced.question: What is net?
options: Low-level network: TCP, UDP, Unix sockets
advanced.explanation: conn, _ := net.Dial("tcp", "host:port"); conn.Write([]byte(data))

advanced.question: What is net.Listener?
advanced.explanation: for { conn, _ := ln.Accept(); go handleConn(conn) } accepts clients.

advanced.question: What is TLS?
options: Transport Layer Security: crypto/tls for secure connections
advanced.explanation: tls.Config{Certificates: []tls.Certificate{cert}}; tls.Listen("tcp", ":443", config)

advanced.question: What is acme/autocert?
advanced.explanation: autocert.Manager{Prompt: autocert.AcceptTOS}.Listener() auto-renewes certs.

advanced.question: What is sync.Map?
options: Concurrent map (optimized for append-only or read-heavy)
advanced.explanation: var m sync.Map; m.Store(k, v); v, ok := m.Load(k); m.Range(fn) for iteration.

advanced.question: What is singleflight?
advanced.explanation: singleflight: multiple callers share result; only one triggers fn.

advanced.question: What is errgroup?
advanced.explanation: g, ctx := errgroup.WithContext(ctx); g.Go(func() error { return fn(ctx) })

advanced.question: What is semaphore in Go?
advanced.explanation: sem := semaphore.NewWeighted(5); sem.Acquire(ctx, 2) acquires 2 of 5 weight.

advanced.question: What is ring buffer?
advanced.explanation: r := ring.New(10); r.Value = data; r = r.Next() moves through buffer.

advanced.question: What is heap?
advanced.explanation: h := &MinHeap{}; heap.Push(h, item); item := heap.Pop(h)

advanced.question: What is list?
advanced.explanation: l := list.New(); l.PushBack(v); front := l.Front(); for e := front; e != nil; e = e.Next()

advanced.question: What is sort?
options: sort.Interface: Len, Less, Swap for sorting
advanced.explanation: sort.Slice(s, func(i, j int) bool { return s[i].Age < s[j].Age })

advanced.question: What is sort.Search?
advanced.explanation: i := sort.Search(n, func(i int) bool { return arr[i] >= target })

advanced.question: What is crypto?
options: crypto package: sha256, aes, rsa, tls, x509
advanced.explanation: h := sha256.Sum256([]byte(data)); encrypted, _ := aes.NewCipher(key)

advanced.question: What is hash?
options: hash.Hash interface: Write, Sum, Size, BlockSize
advanced.explanation: h := fn.New(); h.Write(data); hash := h.Sum(nil) computes hash.

advanced.question: What is time?
advanced.explanation: t := time.Now(); dur := t.Sub(start); timer := time.NewTimer(5s)

advanced.question: What is testing/quick?
advanced.explanation: quick.Check(func(x int) bool { return x + 0 == x }, nil) checks property.

advanced.question: What is testing/iotest?
advanced.explanation: r := iotest.HalfReader(reader); all, _ := io.ReadAll(r) tests partial reads.

beginner.question: What is React?
options: A JS library for building user interfaces
beginner.explanation: React is a declarative, component-based UI library by Meta.

beginner.question: What is JSX?
options: JavaScript XML syntax extension for React
beginner.explanation: JSX allows writing HTML-like syntax in JavaScript.

beginner.question: What is a component?
beginner.explanation: Components are independent, reusable UI pieces in React.

beginner.question: What is a functional component?
options: A component defined as a function
A component defined as a class
beginner.explanation: Functional components are plain JS functions returning JSX.

beginner.question: What is props?
beginner.explanation: Props (properties) are read-only arguments passed to components.

beginner.question: What is state?
options: Internal data that changes over time
beginner.explanation: State is mutable data managed within a component.

beginner.question: What is useState?
options: Hook for adding state to functional components
beginner.explanation: useState returns [value, setValue] pair for state management.

beginner.question: How to update state with useState?
options: Call the setter function from useState
beginner.explanation: setValue(newValue) triggers re-render with the new state value.

beginner.question: What is the useEffect hook?
beginner.explanation: useEffect runs after render for effects like data fetching, subscriptions.

beginner.question: When does useEffect run?
beginner.explanation: useEffect runs after paint by default. Provide [] for mount-only.

beginner.question: What is the dependency array in useEffect?
beginner.explanation: [dep] re-runs effect only when dep changes. [] runs once on mount.

beginner.question: What is the cleanup function in useEffect?
options: Runs on unmount or before re-run
beginner.explanation: Return a function from useEffect for cleanup (subscriptions, timers).

beginner.question: What is event handling in React?
beginner.explanation: React uses synthetic events: onClick, onChange, onSubmit.

beginner.question: How to pass event handler as prop?
beginner.explanation: Pass a function reference, not a string or invocation.

beginner.question: What is conditional rendering?
options: Using JS if/&&/ternary to render different UI
beginner.explanation: {condition && <Component />} or {condition ? A : B}

beginner.question: What is key prop in lists?
options: Unique identifier for list items in React
beginner.explanation: Keys help React identify which items changed, added, or removed.

beginner.question: Why avoid index as key?
options: Can cause issues with list reordering
beginner.explanation: Index keys fail when items are reordered, filtered, or inserted.

beginner.question: What is a controlled component?
options: Form input controlled by React state
beginner.explanation: Controlled: value comes from state, changes via onChange handler.

beginner.question: What is an uncontrolled component?
options: Form input managed by DOM itself
beginner.explanation: Use ref to access DOM value directly. Less code but harder to validate.

beginner.question: What is a ref (useRef)?
options: Mutable value that persists across renders
beginner.explanation: useRef returns { current: initialValue }. Changing does not re-render.

beginner.question: What is the Context API?
beginner.explanation: React.createContext() + Provider/Consumer pattern for deep data sharing.

beginner.question: What is useContext hook?
options: Consumes context value in functional components
beginner.explanation: const value = useContext(MyContext) accesses the nearest Provider value.

beginner.question: What is a React Fragment?
options: Wraps elements without adding DOM node
beginner.explanation: <>...</> or <Fragment>...</Fragment> groups children without extra DOM.

beginner.question: What does React.StrictMode do?
beginner.explanation: StrictMode double-invokes effects and renders to detect side effects.

beginner.question: What is lifting state up?
options: Moving shared state to common ancestor
beginner.explanation: Lifting state up shares state between siblings via their parent.

beginner.question: What is props drilling?
options: Passing props through many intermediate components
beginner.explanation: Props drilling passes data through layers that do not need it.

beginner.question: What is children prop?
beginner.explanation: props.children provides access to elements passed between opening/closing tags.

beginner.question: What is defaultProps?
beginner.explanation: Component.defaultProps = { name: "Guest" } sets fallback values.

beginner.question: What is PropTypes?
beginner.explanation: import PropTypes from "prop-types" validates prop types at runtime.

beginner.question: What is the useMemo hook?
beginner.explanation: useMemo(() => expensive(a, b), [a, b]) recomputes only when deps change.

beginner.question: What is the useCallback hook?
beginner.explanation: useCallback(fn, deps) returns the same function reference if deps unchanged.

beginner.question: What is React.memo?
options: Memoizes component output to skip re-renders
beginner.explanation: React.memo(Component) only re-renders if props change (shallow compare).

beginner.question: What is the useReducer hook?
beginner.explanation: useReducer(reducer, initialState) like Redux for local state.

beginner.question: What is the useLayoutEffect hook?
options: Effect that fires synchronously after DOM mutations
beginner.explanation: useLayoutEffect runs before browser paints, useful for measurements.

beginner.question: What is the useId hook?
beginner.explanation: useId() creates stable, unique IDs for HTML attributes.

beginner.question: What is the useTransition hook?
beginner.explanation: useTransition marks updates as low priority, keeping UI responsive.

beginner.question: What is the useDeferredValue hook?
options: Delays updating a value for debounce-like effect
beginner.explanation: useDeferredValue(value) returns a lagging copy for expensive re-renders.

beginner.question: What is the useSyncExternalStore hook?
beginner.explanation: useSyncExternalStore allows external stores to integrate with React 18 concurrent features.

beginner.question: What is the useInsertionEffect hook?
options: Fires before DOM mutations for CSS-in-JS
beginner.explanation: Use sparingly; designed for CSS-in-JS libraries to inject styles.

beginner.question: How does event delegation work in React?
options: React attaches one listener at root
beginner.explanation: React 17+ attaches events to root, not document.

beginner.question: What is SyntheticEvent?
beginner.explanation: SyntheticEvent normalizes event behavior across browsers.

beginner.question: What is the render phase?
options: When React creates elements and reconciles
beginner.explanation: The render phase can be interrupted in concurrent mode.

beginner.question: What is the commit phase?
options: When React applies changes to the DOM
beginner.explanation: The commit phase is synchronous and not interruptible.

beginner.question: What is reconciliation?
options: Algorithm for diffing virtual DOM trees
beginner.explanation: Reconciliation decides which parts of the DOM need updating.

beginner.question: What is the Virtual DOM?
options: Lightweight JS representation of real DOM
beginner.explanation: React uses virtual DOM to minimize expensive real DOM operations.

beginner.question: What is a React portal?
options: Renders children into a different DOM node
beginner.explanation: createPortal(children, domNode) escapes parent DOM hierarchy for modals/tooltips.

beginner.question: What is error boundary?
options: Catches JS errors in child component tree
beginner.explanation: Error boundaries (componentDidCatch) prevent whole app crashes from child errors.

beginner.question: What are Pure Components?
beginner.explanation: React.PureComponent does shallow prop/state comparison to skip re-renders.

beginner.question: What is synthetic event pooling?
options: React reuses event objects for performance (legacy)
beginner.explanation: In React 16, SyntheticEvent objects are pooled. React 17+ no longer pools.

beginner.question: What is Node.js?
options: JavaScript runtime built on Chrome V8 engine
beginner.explanation: Node.js allows running JS on the server side with non-blocking I/O.

intermediate.question: What is the rule of hooks?
options: Hooks must be called at top level, not conditionally
intermediate.explanation: The order of hooks must be consistent across every render.

intermediate.question: Why hooks at top level?
options: React relies on call order to associate state
intermediate.explanation: Hook call order on each render must match for state to be correctly associated.

intermediate.question: What is custom hook?
intermediate.explanation: Custom hooks extract component logic into reusable functions (useXxx naming).

intermediate.question: What is the difference between state and ref?
intermediate.explanation: State changes cause re-render; ref changes do not. Use ref for non-UI values.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between useEffect and useLayoutEffect?
intermediate.explanation: useLayoutEffect blocks painting; useEffect runs after paint.

intermediate.question: What is the cleanup function in useEffect?
options: Runs on unmount or before re-run
intermediate.explanation: Return a function for cleanup to prevent memory leaks.

intermediate.question: How to fetch data with useEffect?
options: Call fetch inside useEffect with [] deps
intermediate.explanation: const [data, setData] = useState(); useEffect(() => { fetch().then(...) }, [])

intermediate.question: What is the race condition in data fetching?
intermediate.explanation: Use cleanup flag or AbortController to ignore stale responses.

intermediate.question: What is the children prop type?
intermediate.explanation: children can be any renderable React content.

intermediate.question: What is render props pattern?
options: Component receives a function as prop to render
intermediate.explanation: Render props share code between components using a prop that is a function.

intermediate.question: What is a higher-order component (HOC)?
options: Function that returns an enhanced component
intermediate.explanation: HOC: const Enhanced = withFeature(WrappedComponent) wraps with extra logic.

intermediate.question: What are controlled vs uncontrolled?
options: Controlled: React state. Uncontrolled: DOM ref.
intermediate.explanation: Controlled inputs use React state; uncontrolled use ref to access DOM value.

intermediate.question: What is the ref callback pattern?
options: Function ref that receives DOM node
intermediate.explanation: Callback ref: <div ref={el => { /* el is DOM node */ }}>

intermediate.question: What is forwardRef?
options: Passes ref through to child component
intermediate.explanation: React.forwardRef((props, ref) => ...) exposes DOM ref from child.

intermediate.question: What is useImperativeHandle?
options: Customizes exposed ref value with forwardRef
intermediate.explanation: useImperativeHandle(ref, () => ({ focus() { ... } })) customizes ref.

intermediate.question: What is code splitting with React?
intermediate.explanation: const LazyComp = React.lazy(() => import("./Comp")) defers loading.

intermediate.question: What is React.lazy?
options: Dynamically imports a component for code splitting
intermediate.explanation: React.lazy takes a dynamic import returning a default-exported component.

intermediate.question: What is Suspense?
options: Shows fallback while lazy components load
intermediate.explanation: <Suspense fallback={<Spinner />}> wraps lazy components.

intermediate.question: What is Suspense for data fetching?
options: Declarative loading states for async data
intermediate.explanation: React 18 Suspense works with data frameworks like Relay and Next.js.

intermediate.question: What is startTransition?
options: Marks state update as non-urgent transition
intermediate.explanation: startTransition(() => setState(...)) keeps UI responsive during large updates.

intermediate.question: What is useTransition?
intermediate.explanation: const [isPending, startTransition] = useTransition() tracks loading.

intermediate.question: What is useDeferredValue?
options: Returns deferred (lagging) version of a value
intermediate.explanation: const deferred = useDeferredValue(value) delays updates for expensive renders.

intermediate.question: What is concurrent mode?
intermediate.explanation: Concurrent features (Suspense, transitions) make React interruptible.

intermediate.question: What is automatic batching?
options: Multiple state updates batched into one re-render
intermediate.explanation: React 18 batches all updates, even in timeouts and promises.

intermediate.question: What is selective hydration?
intermediate.explanation: Next.js hydrates interactive components first for faster time to interactive.

intermediate.question: What is SSR (Server-Side Rendering)?
options: Rendering React to HTML on the server
intermediate.explanation: SSR sends pre-rendered HTML; React hydrates on the client.

intermediate.question: What is hydration?
options: Attaching event handlers to server-rendered HTML
intermediate.explanation: Hydration makes static HTML interactive by running React on the client.

intermediate.question: What is the hydration mismatch?
options: Server HTML differs from client first render
intermediate.explanation: Mismatches happen when server and client render different output.

intermediate.question: What is Next.js?
options: React framework for production with SSR/SSG
intermediate.explanation: Next.js provides SSR, SSG, API routes, file-based routing, and more.

intermediate.question: What are keys used for in React?
options: Identifying list items for efficient reconciliation
intermediate.explanation: Keys help React match elements across re-renders for optimal DOM updates.

intermediate.question: What happens without keys?
options: React may incorrectly reorder or remount items
intermediate.explanation: Without keys, React uses index-based reconciliation which can cause bugs.

intermediate.question: What is flushSync?
intermediate.explanation: ReactDOM.flushSync(() => setState(...)) forces synchronous DOM update.

intermediate.question: What are server components (RSC)?
options: Components that run on the server, never on client
intermediate.explanation: React Server Components reduce bundle size by running on the server.

intermediate.question: What is the "use client" directive?
intermediate.explanation: "use client" in Next.js marks the boundary between server and client components.

intermediate.question: What is the "use server" directive?
intermediate.explanation: "use server" enables calling server functions directly from the client.

intermediate.question: What are server actions?
intermediate.explanation: Server actions simplify form handling and mutations without manual API endpoints.

intermediate.question: What is the cache function in React?
options: Deduplicates and caches async data requests
intermediate.explanation: React.cache() memoizes async functions within the same request scope.

intermediate.question: What is the taintObjectReference?
options: Prevents sensitive objects from being passed to client
intermediate.explanation: taintObjectReference allows marking server-only data that must not leak to client.

intermediate.question: What is the taintUniqueValue?
options: Prevents specific values from reaching the client
intermediate.explanation: taintUniqueValue prevents individual string/number values from passing to client.

intermediate.question: What is Express.js?
intermediate.explanation: Express provides routing, middleware, and HTTP utilities for web apps.

intermediate.question: What is middleware in Express?
options: Functions processing requests before route handler
intermediate.explanation: Middleware (app.use()) modifies req/res, ends request, or calls next().

intermediate.question: What is routing in Express?
intermediate.explanation: app.get("/users", handler), app.post("/users", handler) define routes.

intermediate.question: What is req.params?
intermediate.explanation: GET /users/:id -> req.params.id extracts the route parameter.

intermediate.question: What is req.query?
intermediate.explanation: GET /search?q=hello -> req.query.q returns "hello".

intermediate.question: What is req.body?
intermediate.explanation: req.body contains POST/PUT JSON or form data after parsing middleware.

intermediate.question: What is res.json()?
intermediate.explanation: res.json({ data }) sets Content-Type: application/json and sends.

intermediate.question: What is res.send()?
intermediate.explanation: res.send() automatically sets Content-Type based on the value.

intermediate.question: What is res.status()?
intermediate.explanation: res.status(404).json({ error: "Not found" }) chains with response method.

intermediate.question: What is app.use()?
intermediate.explanation: app.use("/api", router) mounts router at /api prefix.

intermediate.question: What is next() in middleware?
intermediate.explanation: next() calls the next middleware function in the stack.

advanced.question: What is the Fiber architecture?
options: Reconciliation engine reimplemented with linked list tree
advanced.explanation: Fiber enables incremental rendering, error boundaries, and concurrent mode.

advanced.question: What is a Fiber node?
options: Unit of work representing component instance
advanced.explanation: Each Fiber corresponds to a component instance with its work queue.

advanced.question: What is the work loop in React?
options: Processing fiber tree in chunks during idle time
advanced.explanation: The work loop uses requestIdleCallback or postMessage to yield to browser.

advanced.question: What are lanes in React?
advanced.explanation: Lanes assign priority levels (sync > transition > idle) to updates.

advanced.question: What is the React reconciler?
options: Core diffing algorithm comparing virtual DOM trees
advanced.explanation: The reconciler determines which parts of the tree need DOM changes.

advanced.question: What is the stack reconciler?
advanced.explanation: The stack reconciler was synchronous and non-interruptible.

advanced.question: What is the new JSX transform?
options: Automatic JSX transform not requiring React import
advanced.explanation: React 17+ auto-imports jsx() from react/jsx-runtime; no need for import React.

advanced.question: What is the jsx-runtime?
options: Runtime for the new JSX transform
advanced.explanation: The jsx-runtime is automatically used by the compiler for JSX transformation.

advanced.question: What is the act() testing utility?
options: Wraps render/updates for async test assertions
advanced.explanation: act(() => ...) ensures all state updates and effects are processed before assertions.

advanced.question: What is act() in React Testing Library?
options: Ensures all updates processed before assertions
advanced.explanation: Testing Library wraps act() automatically in render(), fireEvent, etc.

advanced.question: What is shallow rendering?
options: Renders only one level of components for unit tests
advanced.explanation: Shallow rendering tests a component in isolation without rendering its children.

advanced.question: What is snapshot testing?
options: Captures component output and compares on re-runs
advanced.explanation: Jest snapshot testing catches unexpected UI changes by comparing serialized output.

advanced.question: What are testing best practices in React?
options: Test behavior, not implementation; use RTL
advanced.explanation: Focus on user-visible behavior; avoid testing internal state or methods.

advanced.question: What is the recommended testing library?
advanced.explanation: RTL encourages testing from user perspective, not implementation details.

advanced.question: What is the getByRole query?
advanced.explanation: getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }) queries by accessible role and name.

advanced.question: What is the priority of queries in RTL?
options: Accessible queries: role > label > text > testid
advanced.explanation: Prefer queries accessible to screen readers for inclusive testing.

advanced.question: What is userEvent vs fireEvent?
advanced.explanation: userEvent dispatches multiple events (click, focus, blur) like a real user.

advanced.question: What is waitFor in RTL?
options: Retries assertion until timeout or success
advanced.explanation: await waitFor(() => expect(...)) handles async state updates.

advanced.question: What is findBy queries?
advanced.explanation: const el = await findByRole("button") waits for element to appear.

advanced.question: What is screen.debug()?
options: Prints DOM as string for debugging
advanced.explanation: screen.debug() outputs the rendered DOM to the console for inspection.

advanced.question: What is the test-utils library?
advanced.explanation: Custom test-utils re-exports RTL with pre-configured providers.

advanced.question: What is MSW (Mock Service Worker)?
options: Intercepts network requests at service worker level
advanced.explanation: MSW enables testing API interactions without mocking fetch/axios.

advanced.question: What is the difference between mount and shallow?
advanced.explanation: Enzyme had shallow (single level) and mount (full DOM with children).

advanced.question: What is the act() in concurrent mode?
options: Flushes and waits for concurrent updates
advanced.explanation: Concurrent act handles async transitions and Suspense in tests.

advanced.question: What is the withContext testing pattern?
advanced.explanation: Render components with their context providers (Router, Theme, etc.).

advanced.question: What is a mocking strategy for hooks?
options: Mock only external dependencies, not React hooks
advanced.explanation: Mock API calls, not useState or useEffect. Test hook behavior through component.

advanced.question: What is the Container/Presentational pattern?
advanced.explanation: Container handles state/logic; presentational renders based on props.

advanced.question: What is component composition?
options: Building complex UI from smaller components
advanced.explanation: Prefer composition over inheritance for reusable and flexible UI architectures.

advanced.question: What is the compound component pattern?
options: Group of related components sharing implicit state
advanced.explanation: Compound components (e.g., <Select><Option>) share context implicitly.

advanced.question: What is the state reducer pattern?
options: Controls state changes via reducer function
advanced.explanation: Downshift uses this pattern: give users control over state transitions.

advanced.question: What is the provider pattern?
advanced.explanation: Provider pattern makes data available to the entire component subtree.

advanced.question: What is the render props pattern?
options: Function as child for sharing logic
advanced.explanation: <DataProvider render={data => <UI {...data} />} /> shares logic.

advanced.question: What is the HOC pattern?
advanced.explanation: withFeature(Wrapped) adds feature by wrapping and returning new component.

advanced.question: What is the hooks pattern?
options: Encapsulating reusable stateful logic in functions
advanced.explanation: Custom hooks replace most render prop and HOC use cases with simpler API.

advanced.question: What is the flux pattern?
advanced.explanation: Flux: action -> dispatcher -> store -> view. Unidirectional makes data flow predictable.

advanced.question: What is Redux?
options: State management library with single store and reducers
advanced.explanation: Redux centralizes state with actions dispatched to pure reducers.

advanced.question: What is the Redux Toolkit?
options: Official Redux toolset simplifying Redux patterns
advanced.explanation: RTK provides configureStore, createSlice, createAsyncThunk, etc.

advanced.question: What is a Redux slice?
options: Reducer + actions bundle for a feature
advanced.explanation: createSlice({name, initialState, reducers}) generates reducer and actions.

advanced.question: What is the middleware in Redux?
advanced.explanation: Redux middleware (thunk, saga, logger) intercepts for side effects and logging.

advanced.question: What is Redux Thunk?
advanced.explanation: Thunk allows action creators to return functions instead of plain action objects.

advanced.question: What is Redux Saga?
options: Middleware using generators for side effects
advanced.explanation: Sagas use ES6 generators to handle complex async flows declaratively.

advanced.question: What is the selector in Redux?
options: Function extracting derived data from store
advanced.explanation: Selectors (useSelector) efficiently extract and memoize state from store.

advanced.question: What is the Zustand library?
options: Tiny, fast state management with hooks API
advanced.explanation: Zustand provides a minimal hook-based state store without boilerplate.

advanced.question: What is Jotai?
options: Atomic state management with primitive atoms
advanced.explanation: Jotai models state as independent atoms that can be composed.

advanced.question: What is Recoil?
advanced.explanation: Recoil provides atoms and selectors with data-flow graph for shared state.

advanced.question: What is the Node.js event loop phases?
options: timers -> I/O callbacks -> idle -> poll -> check -> close
advanced.explanation: The event loop has 6 phases; each phase has a FIFO queue of callbacks.

advanced.question: What is the poll phase?
options: Retrieves new I/O events and executes callbacks
advanced.explanation: The poll phase blocks and waits for I/O if no timers are pending.

advanced.question: What is the check phase?
advanced.explanation: The check phase runs after poll if setImmediate() is scheduled.

advanced.question: What is the close phase?
advanced.explanation: Close callbacks (socket.on("close", ...)) fire in this phase.

advanced.question: What is libuv?
options: C library for async I/O in Node.js
advanced.explanation: libuv provides the event loop, thread pool, and async I/O across platforms.

beginner.question: What is TypeScript?
options: Typed superset of JavaScript compiling to JS
beginner.explanation: TypeScript adds static types, interfaces, generics, and tooling to JS.

beginner.question: What are basic TS types?
options: number, string, boolean, null, undefined, void
beginner.explanation: TS extends JS with any, unknown, never, enum, tuple.

beginner.question: How to type a variable?
beginner.explanation: Type annotations use colon syntax: variable: Type = value.

beginner.question: What is the any type?
beginner.explanation: any disables type checking; use sparingly as it defeats TypeScript's purpose.

beginner.question: What is the unknown type?
options: Type-safe counterpart, must check before use
beginner.explanation: unknown requires type narrowing before use, unlike any.

beginner.question: What is void type?
beginner.explanation: void indicates a function has no return value (returns undefined).

beginner.question: What is never type?
options: Type for values that never occur
beginner.explanation: never represents unreachable code (e.g., always-throwing function).

beginner.question: What is the null type?
beginner.explanation: In strict mode, null is its own type. Use union types like string | null.

beginner.question: What is the undefined type?
beginner.explanation: In strict mode, undefined is its own type. Use union types.

beginner.question: What is type annotation?
beginner.explanation: Type annotations use : TypeName after the variable name.

beginner.question: What is type inference?
beginner.explanation: TS infers types from initial values: let x = 5 infers number.

beginner.question: What is an interface?
options: Defines object shape with properties and methods
beginner.explanation: interface Person { name: string; age: number } defines an object contract.

beginner.question: What is a type alias?
options: Creates a name for a type
beginner.explanation: type Point = { x: number; y: number } creates a type greeting.

beginner.question: Difference between interface and type?
options: interface can be extended, type cannot (mostly)
beginner.explanation: interface extends via extends; type uses intersection (&).

beginner.question: What is optional property (?)?
beginner.explanation: interface Config { name?: string } means name is optional.

beginner.question: What is readonly property?
beginner.explanation: readonly name: string prevents reassignment after initialization.

beginner.question: What is an array type?
beginner.explanation: Both number[] and Array<number> represent arrays of numbers.

beginner.question: What is a tuple type?
beginner.explanation: let pair: [string, number] = ["age", 30] is a tuple.

beginner.question: What is an enum?
beginner.explanation: enum Color { Red, Green, Blue } defines numeric values starting at 0.

beginner.question: What is a union type?
options: Value can be one of several types
beginner.explanation: string | number means the value can be either a string or a number.

beginner.question: What is an intersection type?
beginner.explanation: type A = B & C means A has all properties of B and C.

beginner.question: What is a literal type?
beginner.explanation: let status: "active" | "inactive" restricts to exact strings.

beginner.question: What is type assertion?
options: Telling TS the type when you know better
beginner.explanation: value as string or <string>value asserts the type to TS.

beginner.question: What is the non-null assertion (!)?
options: Tells TS value is not null/undefined
beginner.explanation: name!.length asserts that name is not null.

beginner.question: What is a function type?
options: Type describing function parameters and return
beginner.explanation: type Fn = (x: number) => string describes a function type.

beginner.question: What is optional parameter (?)?
beginner.explanation: function greet(name?: string) makes the parameter optional.

beginner.question: What is default parameter?
options: Parameter with default value if omitted
beginner.explanation: function greet(name = "Guest") uses "Guest" if no argument given.

beginner.question: What is rest parameter (...)?
beginner.explanation: function sum(...nums: number[]) captures all arguments.

beginner.question: What is function overload?
beginner.explanation: Overloads define multiple call signatures with different types.

beginner.question: What is a generic function?
options: Function that works with any type
beginner.explanation: function identity<T>(arg: T): T { return arg } works with any type.

beginner.question: What is a generic constraint?
beginner.explanation: function fn<T extends Lengthwise>(arg: T) requires T to have .length.

beginner.question: What is keyof operator?
options: Union of keys of a type
beginner.explanation: keyof Person returns "name" | "age" for Person type.

beginner.question: What is typeof operator?
options: Gets the type of a value
beginner.explanation: typeof obj returns the TypeScript type of the value.

beginner.question: What is indexed access type?
beginner.explanation: type Name = Person["name"] extracts the type of name property.

beginner.question: What is conditional type?
options: Type that depends on a condition
beginner.explanation: type IsString<T> = T extends string ? "yes" : "no"

beginner.question: What is infer keyword?
beginner.explanation: type Return<T> = T extends (...args: any[]) => infer R ? R : never;

beginner.question: What is mapped type?
beginner.explanation: type Readonly<T> = { readonly [P in keyof T]: T[P] }

beginner.question: What is Partial<T>?
beginner.explanation: Partial<Config> makes every property in Config optional.

beginner.question: What is Required<T>?
beginner.explanation: Required<Config> makes every property in Config required.

beginner.question: What is Readonly<T>?
beginner.explanation: Readonly<Config> prevents reassignment of any property.

beginner.question: What is Pick<T, K>?
options: Creates type with only specified keys
beginner.explanation: Pick<Person, "name" | "age"> selects only those properties.

beginner.question: What is Omit<T, K>?
beginner.explanation: Omit<Person, "password"> excludes the password property.

beginner.question: What is Record<K, V>?
options: Creates type with keys K and values V
beginner.explanation: Record<"a" | "b", number> = { a: number; b: number }

beginner.question: What is Exclude<T, U>?
beginner.explanation: Exclude<string | number, string> = number.

beginner.question: What is Extract<T, U>?
beginner.explanation: Extract<string | number, string> = string.

beginner.question: What is NonNullable<T>?
options: Removes null and undefined from type
beginner.explanation: NonNullable<string | null> = string.

beginner.question: What is ReturnType<T>?
options: Gets the return type of function
beginner.explanation: ReturnType<typeof fn> extracts the function's return type.

beginner.question: What is Parameters<T>?
beginner.explanation: Parameters<typeof fn> extracts parameter types as a tuple.

beginner.question: What is ConstructorParameters<T>?
options: Gets tuple of constructor parameter types
beginner.explanation: ConstructorParameters<typeof Class> gets constructor arg types.

beginner.question: What is InstanceType<T>?
options: Gets the instance type of a constructor
beginner.explanation: InstanceType<typeof MyClass> is the class instance type.

intermediate.question: What is a class in TypeScript?
options: Class with typed properties and visibility
intermediate.explanation: TS classes support public, private, protected, readonly, and parameter properties.

intermediate.question: What is public in TS?
intermediate.explanation: public is the default visibility; accessible from anywhere.

intermediate.question: What is private in TS?
intermediate.explanation: private restricts access to the class itself (not JS # private).

intermediate.question: What is protected in TS?
intermediate.explanation: protected allows access within the class and its subclasses.

intermediate.question: What are parameter properties?
options: Declaring and initializing in constructor params
intermediate.explanation: constructor(public name: string) declares and assigns name in one step.

intermediate.question: What is abstract class?
intermediate.explanation: abstract class cannot be instantiated; subclasses implement abstract methods.

intermediate.question: What is abstract method?
options: Method without body, must be implemented in subclass
intermediate.explanation: abstract method() : void; forces subclasses to provide implementation.

intermediate.question: What is implements keyword?
options: Class commits to a specific interface
intermediate.explanation: class MyClass implements MyInterface ensures the interface contract.

intermediate.question: What is a namespace?
intermediate.explanation: Namespaces are internal modules; prefer ES modules for modern code.

intermediate.question: What is a module in TS?
intermediate.explanation: Any file with top-level import/export is a module; others are scripts.

intermediate.question: What is ambient declaration?
options: Declares types for existing JS code
intermediate.explanation: declare module "module-name" or .d.ts files declare external types.

intermediate.question: What is a .d.ts file?
options: Declaration file describing JS module types
intermediate.explanation: .d.ts files contain only type declarations, no implementation.

intermediate.question: What is triple-slash directive?
intermediate.explanation: /// <reference path="..." /> references other declaration files.

intermediate.question: What is tsconfig.json?
intermediate.explanation: tsconfig.json specifies compiler options, files, and project settings.

intermediate.question: What does strict mode enable?
intermediate.explanation: strict: true enables noImplicitAny, strictNullChecks, etc.

intermediate.question: What does strictNullChecks do?
options: null/undefined are only assignable to their types
intermediate.explanation: With strictNullChecks, string | null must be explicitly nullable.

intermediate.question: What does noImplicitAny do?
options: Errors when type cannot be inferred
intermediate.explanation: noImplicitAny reports an error when TS cannot infer a type.

intermediate.question: What does noUnusedLocals do?
intermediate.explanation: Keeps code clean by erroring on declared but unused local variables.

intermediate.question: What does noUnusedParameters do?
intermediate.explanation: Prevents dead parameters in function signatures.

intermediate.question: What does strictFunctionTypes do?
intermediate.explanation: Ensures function type compatibility is checked contravariantly.

intermediate.question: What does strictPropertyInitialization do?
intermediate.explanation: Ensures every class property has a value in the constructor.

intermediate.question: What is declaration merging?
options: Multiple declarations of same name merge
intermediate.explanation: Interfaces with the same name merge their members automatically.

intermediate.question: What is module augmentation?
intermediate.explanation: declare module "lib" { ... } extends the module's type declarations.

intermediate.question: What is global augmentation?
intermediate.explanation: declare global { ... } adds types to the global scope from a module.

intermediate.question: What is const assertion (as const)?
options: Makes value deeply readonly literal type
intermediate.explanation: const x = { a: 1 } as const makes x.a type 1, not number.

intermediate.question: What is satisfies operator?
intermediate.explanation: const x = { a: 1 } satisfies Record<string, number> keeps narrow type.

intermediate.question: What is a type guard?
options: Runtime check that narrows type in scope
intermediate.explanation: typeof, instanceof, or custom functions can narrow union types.

intermediate.question: What is typeof type guard?
intermediate.explanation: typeof narrows primitive unions: typeof x === "number" narrows to number.

intermediate.question: What is instanceof type guard?
options: Checks if value is instance of class
intermediate.explanation: x instanceof Date narrows the type to Date in the if block.

intermediate.question: What is the in operator type guard?
options: Checks if property exists on object
intermediate.explanation: "prop" in obj narrows to types with that property.

intermediate.question: What is a discriminated union?
options: Union with common literal property for narrowing
intermediate.explanation: type Shape = { kind: "circle"; r: number } | { kind: "square"; side: number }

intermediate.question: What is exhaustive checking?
options: Ensuring all union cases are handled
intermediate.explanation: The never type helps: default: const _exhaustive: never = x catches missed cases.

intermediate.question: What is template literal type?
intermediate.explanation: type EventName = `on${Capitalize<string>}` creates "onClick" etc.

intermediate.question: What is intrinsic string type?
intermediate.explanation: Capitalize<"hello"> returns "Hello" type.

intermediate.question: What is the as const suffix?
intermediate.explanation: const arr = [1, 2, 3] as const gives readonly [1, 2, 3] type.

intermediate.question: What is branded type?
options: Unique type with symbol to prevent assignment
intermediate.explanation: type Brand<T, B> = T & { __brand: B } creates distinct types.

intermediate.question: What is satisfies vs : Type?
intermediate.explanation: satisfies ensures type compatibility while keeping the original inferred type.

intermediate.question: What is the globalThis type?
options: Type for the global object across environments
intermediate.explanation: globalThis is standard for accessing globals in any JS runtime.

intermediate.question: What is the AsyncGenerator type?
intermediate.explanation: AsyncGenerator<T, TReturn, TNext> types async generators.

intermediate.question: What is the IterableIterator type?
intermediate.explanation: IterableIterator<T> is returned by generator functions.

intermediate.question: What is the Awaited<T> type?
intermediate.explanation: Awaited<Promise<Promise<string>>> = string.

intermediate.question: What is the NoInfer<T>?
intermediate.explanation: NoInfer<T> is a utility to prevent TS from inferring from that argument.

intermediate.question: What is Express.js?
intermediate.explanation: Express provides routing, middleware, and HTTP utilities for web apps.

intermediate.question: What is middleware in Express?
options: Functions processing requests before route handler
intermediate.explanation: Middleware (app.use()) modifies req/res, ends request, or calls next().

intermediate.question: What is routing in Express?
intermediate.explanation: app.get("/users", handler), app.post("/users", handler) define routes.

intermediate.question: What is req.params?
intermediate.explanation: GET /users/:id -> req.params.id extracts the route parameter.

intermediate.question: What is req.query?
intermediate.explanation: GET /search?q=hello -> req.query.q returns "hello".

intermediate.question: What is req.body?
intermediate.explanation: req.body contains POST/PUT JSON or form data after parsing middleware.

intermediate.question: What is res.json()?
intermediate.explanation: res.json({ data }) sets Content-Type: application/json and sends.

intermediate.question: What is res.send()?
intermediate.explanation: res.send() automatically sets Content-Type based on the value.

advanced.question: What is covariance?
options: Preserves type relationship in same direction
advanced.explanation: Arrays are covariant: Dog[] is assignable to Animal[].

advanced.question: What is contravariance?
advanced.explanation: Function parameters are contravariant: (Animal) => void accepts (Dog) => void.

advanced.question: What is structural typing?
options: Compatible if same shape, not nominal name
advanced.explanation: TS uses structural typing: { a: 1 } is compatible with { a: number }.

advanced.question: What is nominal typing?
options: Compatible only if same explicit type name
advanced.explanation: TypeScript does not use nominal typing; types are structurally compared.

advanced.question: What is the monad pattern in TS?
advanced.explanation: Monads like Option/Option<T> wrap values for safe chaining.

advanced.question: What is the builder pattern in TS?
options: Fluent object construction with typed steps
advanced.explanation: class Builder { step1(): this { ... } step2(): this { ... } } enables chaining.

advanced.question: What is a declaration file (.d.ts)?
options: Describes JS library types for TS
advanced.explanation: declare module "lib" { export function fn(): void } describes types.

advanced.question: What is @types or DefinitelyTyped?
options: Community type definitions for npm packages
advanced.explanation: @types/package provides community-maintained type declarations.

advanced.question: What is project references?
advanced.explanation: Project references enable incremental builds and type-checking across sub-projects.

advanced.question: What is incremental compilation?
advanced.explanation: tsc --incremental saves .tsbuildinfo for faster subsequent builds.

advanced.question: What is isolatedModules?
advanced.explanation: Ensures each module can be transpiled independently without cross-file type info.

advanced.question: What is verbatimModuleSyntax?
advanced.explanation: Ensures import/export syntax is not elided or rewritten.

advanced.question: What is the import type syntax?
options: Imports only the type, erased at runtime
advanced.explanation: import type { Foo } from "./foo" is completely removed in output.

advanced.question: What is the export type syntax?
advanced.explanation: export type { Foo } re-exports only the type, not the value.

advanced.question: What are decorators in TS?
advanced.explanation: Decorators @logMethod add behavior to classes, methods, or properties.

advanced.question: What is experimentalDecorators?
advanced.explanation: Legacy decorators (TC39 stage 2) require experimentalDecorators: true.

advanced.question: What is emitDecoratorMetadata?
advanced.explanation: emitDecoratorMetadata enables reflect-metadata for design-time type info.

advanced.question: What is ts-node?
options: Runs TS files directly in Node.js
advanced.explanation: ts-node compiles and executes TS in Node without a separate build step.

advanced.question: What is esModuleInterop?
advanced.explanation: Enables default imports from CJS modules that export as namespace.

advanced.question: What is allowSyntheticDefaultImports?
options: Allows default imports from CJS without default export
advanced.explanation: Allows import React from "react" even without default export.

advanced.question: What is resolveJsonModule?
advanced.explanation: Enables import data from "./data.json" with automatic typing.

advanced.question: What is skipLibCheck?
advanced.explanation: skipLibCheck: true speeds up compilation by skipping library type checks.

advanced.question: What is downlevelIteration?
advanced.explanation: Provides full iteration support for for...of when targeting older ES versions.

advanced.question: What is importHelpers?
advanced.explanation: Reduces output size by importing shared helpers from tslib.

advanced.question: What is paths in tsconfig?
options: Maps module import paths to directories
advanced.explanation: "paths": { "@/*": ["./src/*"] } enables clean imports like import x from "@/utils".

advanced.question: What is baseUrl in tsconfig?
options: Base directory for resolving non-relative paths
advanced.explanation: baseUrl sets the root for module resolution when using non-relative imports.

advanced.question: What is outDir in tsconfig?
options: Output directory for compiled JS files
advanced.explanation: outDir: "./dist" places all compiled files under the dist folder.

advanced.question: What is rootDir in tsconfig?
advanced.explanation: rootDir controls which files are included and how output structure is mirrored.

advanced.question: What is composite in tsconfig?
options: Enables project references for incremental builds
advanced.explanation: composite: true declares a project as referenceable for project references.

advanced.question: What is declaration in tsconfig?
advanced.explanation: declaration: true produces .d.ts files for library distribution.
