all.explanation: C++ supports multiple inheritance; Java/C# use interfaces instead.

all.question: What is multi-level inheritance?
all.explanation: Grandparent -> Parent -> Child forms a chain of inheritance.

all.question: What is hierarchical inheritance?
all.explanation: Animal parent: Dog, Cat, Bird all extend Animal.

all.question: What is hybrid inheritance?
options: Combination of multiple and multi-level inheritance
all.explanation: Hybrid can cause diamond problem in C++; resolved with virtual inheritance.

all.question: What is polymorphism?
options: Same method behaves differently based on object
all.explanation: Polymorphism: Animal.sound() -> Dog barks, Cat meows.

all.question: What is compile-time polymorphism?
all.explanation: Overloaded methods: add(int, int), add(double, double) resolved at compile time.

all.question: What is runtime polymorphism?
all.explanation: Parent reference, child object: Animal a = new Dog(); a.sound() calls Dog.sound().

all.question: What is method overloading?
options: Same name, different parameters within a class
all.explanation: Overloading: add(int a), add(int a, int b) -> same name, different params.

all.question: What is method overriding?
all.explanation: Override: @Override void display() in child vs void display() in parent.

all.question: What is virtual function?
all.explanation: virtual void sound() = 0; derived classes must override pure virtual.

all.question: What is an abstract class?
options: Cannot instantiate; may have abstract methods
all.explanation: abstract class Animal { abstract void sound(); } must be subclassed.

all.question: What is an interface?
all.explanation: interface Drawable { void draw(); } classes implement interface methods.

all.question: What is difference: abstract vs interface?
options: Abstract: can have state/constructors; Interface: pure contract
all.explanation: Interface: all methods abstract, no state (until Java 8 default methods).

all.question: What is this keyword?
all.explanation: this refers to the current object; used to disambiguate fields.

all.question: What is super keyword?
all.explanation: super() calls parent constructor; super.method() calls parent method.

all.question: What is a constructor?
options: Special method called when object is created
all.explanation: Constructor initializes object; has same name as class; no return type.

all.question: What is default constructor?
options: Compiler-generated no-arg constructor if none defined
all.explanation: If no constructor defined, compiler provides empty default constructor.

all.question: What is parameterized constructor?
all.explanation: class Person(String name, int age) { this.name = name; this.age = age; }

all.question: What is copy constructor?
options: Creates new object as copy of existing object
all.explanation: Person(Person other) { this.name = other.name; this.age = other.age; }

all.question: What is destructor?
all.explanation: C++: ~ClassName(); Java: finalize() (deprecated); C#: ~ClassName() finalizer.

all.question: What is access modifier?
options: Controls visibility: public, private, protected, default
all.explanation: public: all; private: class only; protected: class + subclasses; default: package.

all.question: What is public?
all.explanation: public fields/methods are part of the class API.

all.question: What is private?
options: Accessible only within the same class
all.explanation: private enforces encapsulation; accessed via getters/setters.

all.question: What is protected?
all.explanation: protected balances encapsulation with inheritance access.

all.question: What is static?
all.explanation: static int count; accessed as ClassName.count; shared across all instances.

all.question: What is static method?
options: Called on class, not instance; no access to instance variables
all.explanation: Math.sqrt(25) static; cannot access this or super.

all.question: What is static block?
options: Runs once when class is loaded
all.explanation: static { System.out.println("Class loaded"); } runs at class initialization.

all.question: What is final keyword?
options: Cannot change: final class, method, variable
all.explanation: final class cannot extend; final method cannot override; final var is constant.

all.question: What is a constant?
all.explanation: Constants are static final: public static final double PI = 3.14159;

all.question: What is a static initializer?
options: Static block executed when class is loaded
all.explanation: static { config = loadConfig(); } runs once per class.

all.question: What is an inner class?
all.explanation: Inner class has access to outer class members (including private).

all.question: What is a static nested class?
all.explanation: Static nested class behaves like top-level; no access to outer instance.

all.question: What is an anonymous class?
options: Class without name, defined and instantiated on the fly
all.explanation: button.addActionListener(new ActionListener() { public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { ... } })

all.question: What is a lambda?
all.explanation: (int x, int y) -> x + y; single-method interfaces become lambdas.

all.question: What is functional interface?
options: Interface with exactly one abstract method
all.explanation: @FunctionalInterface interface Predicate<T> { boolean test(T t); }

all.question: What is method reference?
all.explanation: list.forEach(System.out::println) is shorthand for x -> System.out.println(x).

all.question: What is garbage collection?
options: Automatic memory reclamation for unused objects
all.explanation: GC identifies unreachable objects and frees their memory.

all.question: What is a reference type?
all.explanation: Reference types: class, interface, array, String; stored in heap.

all.question: What is a value type?
options: Variable holds actual data; stored on stack
all.explanation: Value types: int, float, boolean, char; passed by value.

all.question: What is autoboxing?
all.explanation: Autoboxing: Integer i = 42; compiler inserts Integer.valueOf(42).

all.question: What is unboxing?
all.explanation: Unboxing: int x = i; compiler inserts i.intValue().

all.question: What is pass-by-value?
options: Method receives copy of value (Java, C#)
all.explanation: In pass-by-value, method cannot change caller's variable (for primitives).

all.question: What is pass-by-reference?
options: Method receives reference to variable (C++, C# ref)
all.explanation: C++: void swap(int& a, int& b) modifies caller variables.

all.question: What is composition?
options: HAS-A relationship: class Car { Engine e; }
all.explanation: Composition: Car owns Engine; Engine cannot exist without Car.

all.question: What is aggregation?
options: HAS-A with independent lifecycle: class Team { List<Player> }
all.explanation: Aggregation: Players can exist without Team (weak ownership).

all.question: What is association?
options: Uses-A relationship: class Driver { void drive(Car c) }
all.explanation: Association: Driver uses Car temporarily via method parameter.

all.question: What is a package?
all.explanation: Package: com.example.myapp; prevents naming conflicts.

all.question: What is a module?
options: Group of packages with explicit dependencies (Java 9+)
all.explanation: module-info.java: module myapp { requires java.sql; exports com.example.api; }

all.question: What is SOLID?
all.explanation: SOLID: Single Responsibility, Open-Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion.

all.question: What is Single Responsibility?
options: Class should have one reason to change
all.explanation: SRP: Report class only formats; separate Database class for persistence.

all.question: What is Open-Closed?
options: Open for extension, closed for modification
all.explanation: OCP: extend via subclass/interface impl, not by modifying existing code.

all.question: What is Liskov Substitution?
options: Subtypes must be substitutable for base type
all.explanation: LSP: Rectangle r = new Square() should work; Square extends Rectangle violates LSP.

all.question: What is Interface Segregation?
options: Many specific interfaces > one general interface
all.explanation: ISP: Worker interface split into Workable, Eatable; Robot only implements Workable.

all.question: What is Dependency Inversion?
all.explanation: DIP: High-level module depends on Storage interface, not FileStorage directly.

all.question: What is DRY?
options: Don't Repeat Yourself: avoid duplicate code
all.explanation: DRY: Extract repeated logic into reusable methods/classes/modules.

all.question: What is KISS?
options: Keep It Simple, Stupid: simplicity over complexity
all.explanation: KISS: Prefer simple solutions; avoid unnecessary complexity.

all.question: What is YAGNI?
options: You Ain't Gonna Need It: don't add unused functionality
all.explanation: YAGNI: implement only what is required now, not anticipated future needs.

all.question: What is a design pattern?
options: Reusable solution to common software problem
all.explanation: Design patterns: proven solutions categorized as creational, structural, behavioral.

all.question: What is Singleton?
options: Ensures class has only one instance globally
all.explanation: Singleton: private constructor, static getInstance(), lazy or eager init.

all.question: What is Factory?
options: Creates objects without specifying concrete class
all.explanation: Factory: ShapeFactory.create("circle") returns new Circle().

all.question: What is Abstract Factory?
all.explanation: AbstractFactory: GUIFactory.createButton() -> WindowsButton or MacButton.

all.question: What is Builder?
options: Constructs complex objects step by step
all.explanation: Builder: new PizzaBuilder().setSize("large").addTopping("cheese").build()

all.question: What is Prototype?
options: Creates objects by cloning existing ones
all.explanation: Prototype: clone() for expensive-to-create objects.

all.question: What is Adapter?
options: Converts interface to another expected interface
all.explanation: Adapter: LegacyPrinter implementing ModernPrinter interface via delegation.

all.question: What is Bridge?
all.explanation: Bridge: Shape has DrawingAPI; Circle draws with DrawingAPI.drawCircle().

all.question: What is Composite?
options: Treats individual and composite objects uniformly
all.explanation: Composite: File and Directory both implement FileSystemNode with getSize().

all.question: What is Decorator?
all.explanation: Decorator: DataSource -> CompressionDecorator -> EncryptionDecorator.

all.question: What is Facade?
all.explanation: Facade: ComputerFacade { cpu.start(); memory.load(); hdd.read(); }

all.question: What is Proxy?
all.explanation: Proxy: ImageProxy { RealImage real; display() { if (real == null) real = new RealImage(); real.display(); } }

all.question: What is Chain of Responsibility?
all.explanation: CoR: Logger chain: Debug -> Info -> Error; each logs or passes to next.

all.question: What is Command?
all.explanation: Command: Button invokes Command.execute(); CommandHistory supports undo.

all.question: What is Iterator?
all.explanation: Iterator: for (Item item : collection) { ... } using iterator() and next().

all.question: What is Mediator?
all.explanation: Mediator: ChatRoom mediates messages between User objects.

all.question: What is Memento?
all.explanation: Memento: Editor -> createMemento() saves state; setMemento(m) restores.

all.question: What is Observer?
options: Subject notifies observers on state change
all.explanation: Observer: Subject.attach(observer); Subject.notify() calls update on all.

all.question: What is State?
options: Changes behavior based on internal state
all.explanation: State: VendingMachine has states: NoCoin, HasCoin, Dispensing; each handles actions differently.

all.question: What is Strategy?
all.explanation: Strategy: PaymentStrategy interface; CreditCard, PayPal implement pay().

all.question: What is Template Method?
options: Defines skeleton of algorithm; subclasses implement steps
all.explanation: Template: DataMiner { abstract void extract(); void process() { extract(); parse(); analyze(); } }

all.question: What is Visitor?
all.explanation: Visitor: Shape.accept(Visitor); Visitor.visit(Circle), visit(Rectangle).

all.question: What is composition over inheritance?
options: Prefer has-a over is-a for flexibility
all.explanation: Composition: class Car { List<Wheel> wheels; } vs Car extends Vehicle.

all.question: What is object cloning?
all.explanation: clone() (Java shallow); deep copy: serialize/deserialize or manual copy.

all.question: What is serialization?
options: Converting object to byte stream for storage/transmission
all.explanation: Serializable: marker interface; ObjectOutputStream.writeObject(obj).

all.question: What is deserialization?
all.explanation: ObjectInputStream.readObject() reconstructs object from stream.

all.question: What is reflection in OOP?
all.explanation: Class.forName("MyClass").getMethod("doIt").invoke(obj) for dynamic invocation.

all.question: What is polymorphic dispatch?
all.explanation: Virtual method table (vtable) finds correct override at runtime.

all.question: What is a virtual table?
options: Table mapping overridable methods to actual implementations
all.explanation: Each class has vtable; object has vpointer to class vtable.

all.question: What is covariance?
options: Return type can be more specific in override
all.explanation: Java: Dog getAnimal() overriding Animal getAnimal() is covariant return.

all.question: What is contravariance?
options: Parameter type can be more general (opposite direction)
all.explanation: Comparable<Object> can compare any type; Comparable<Dog> only dogs.

all.question: What is generics?
all.explanation: Generics provide compile-time type safety: List<String> only accepts strings.

all.question: What is type erasure?
all.explanation: Java: List<String> becomes List (raw) at runtime; C# preserves generic info.

all.question: What is reified generics?
options: Type info available at runtime (C#, Kotlin)
all.explanation: C#: List<int> at runtime knows it holds ints; no boxing for value types.

all.question: What is wildcard?
all.explanation: ? extends T (upper bound), ? super T (lower bound), ? (unbounded).

all.question: What is a data structure?
options: Way to organize and store data for efficient access
all.explanation: Data structures: arrays, linked lists, trees, graphs, hash tables, stacks, queues.

all.question: What is an array?
options: Contiguous memory, fixed size, O(1) index access
all.explanation: Arrays store elements of same type at adjacent memory locations.

all.question: What is a dynamic array?
all.explanation: Dynamic array doubles capacity when full; amortized O(1) append.

all.question: What is a linked list?
options: Nodes with data and pointer to next node
all.explanation: Linked list: O(1) insert/delete at known position; O(n) access.

all.question: What is singly linked list?
options: Each node points to next only; forward traversal
all.explanation: Singly linked: head node; last node points to null.

all.question: What is doubly linked list?
options: Nodes point to both next and previous
all.explanation: Doubly linked: traversal both directions; prev pointer extra memory.

all.question: What is circular linked list?
options: Last node points to first node (loop)
all.explanation: Circular: useful for round-robin scheduling; no null end.

all.question: What is a stack?
options: LIFO: Last In, First Out data structure
all.explanation: Stack: push/pop O(1); used in function calls, expression evaluation, undo.

all.question: What is a queue?
options: FIFO: First In, First Out data structure
all.explanation: Queue: enqueue/dequeue O(1); used in BFS, printing, buffering.

all.question: What is a deque?
all.explanation: Deque: push_front, push_back, pop_front, pop_back all O(1).

all.question: What is a priority queue?
options: Elements dequeued by priority (not insertion order)
all.explanation: Priority queue: max/min heap; highest/lowest priority out first.

all.question: What is a hash table?
options: Key-value store with O(1) average lookup
all.explanation: Hash table: hash function maps key to bucket; collision resolution.

all.question: What is a hash function?
options: Maps arbitrary key to integer index in hash table
all.explanation: Good hash: uniform distribution, fast computation, deterministic.

all.question: What is a collision?
options: Two keys hash to same bucket
all.explanation: Collision resolution: chaining (linked list per bucket) or open addressing.

all.question: What is chaining?
options: Collision resolution: linked list at each hash bucket
all.explanation: Chaining: simple, no limit on elements per bucket; memory overhead for pointers.

all.question: What is open addressing?
options: Find next empty slot on collision (probing)
all.explanation: Open addressing: linear probing, quadratic probing, double hashing.

all.question: What is linear probing?
all.explanation: Linear: simple, but primary clustering (long runs of occupied slots).

all.question: What is quadratic probing?
all.explanation: Quadratic: reduces clustering; may not find all slots.

all.question: What is double hashing?
all.explanation: Double: hash2(key) stride; uniform probe sequence.

all.question: What is a set?
all.explanation: Set: hash set (O(1)), tree set (O(log n)); union, intersect, difference.

all.question: What is a map/dictionary?
options: Key-value pairs: each key maps to one value
all.explanation: Map: hash map (O(1)), tree map (O(log n)); keys unique.

all.question: What is a tree?
options: Hierarchical structure: root, nodes, edges, leaves
all.explanation: Tree: root with children; no cycles; binary tree has 0-2 children per node.

all.question: What is binary tree?
options: Each node has at most 2 children (left, right)
all.explanation: Binary tree: left subtree, right subtree; used for BST, heap.

all.question: What is a binary search tree?
options: Left < parent < right invariant for all nodes
all.explanation: BST: inorder traversal yields sorted order; O(log n) avg search.

all.question: What is tree traversal (preorder)?
all.explanation: Preorder: used in tree copy, prefix expression, serialization.

all.question: What is tree traversal (inorder)?
all.explanation: Inorder: on BST gives sorted sequence; ascending order.

all.question: What is tree traversal (postorder)?
all.explanation: Postorder: used in tree deletion, expression evaluation.

all.question: What is level-order traversal?
options: BFS: visit nodes level by level
all.explanation: Level-order: use queue; visit all nodes at depth d before d+1.

all.question: What is a balanced tree?
all.explanation: AVL: balance factor -1,0,1; Red-Black: color constraints; ensures O(log n).

all.question: What is an AVL tree?
options: Self-balancing BST: heights differ at most 1
all.explanation: AVL: rotations (LL, RR, LR, RL) maintain balance after insert/delete.

all.question: What is a Red-Black tree?
all.explanation: RB: root black, no adjacent reds, equal black height; less strict than AVL.

all.question: What is a B-tree?
options: Multi-way balanced tree optimized for disk
all.explanation: B-tree: each node has many children (keys); minimizes I/O for databases.

all.question: What is a B+ tree?
options: B-tree variant: all data in leaves; linked leaf nodes
all.explanation: B+: interior nodes contain only keys; leaves have data + next pointer.

all.question: What is a trie?
all.explanation: Trie: each node represents character; O(m) search/insert for m-length string.

all.question: What is a heap?
all.explanation: Heap: array representation; left[i]=2i+1, right[i]=2i+2.

all.question: What is a max-heap?
options: Parent >= children; root is maximum element
all.explanation: Max-heap: extract max O(log n); used in heap sort.

all.question: What is a min-heap?
options: Parent <= children; root is minimum element
all.explanation: Min-heap: extract min O(log n); used in priority queue.

all.question: What is heapify?
options: Building heap from array: O(n) bottom-up
all.explanation: heapify: sift-down starting from last non-leaf node to root.

all.question: What is a graph?
all.explanation: Graph: directed/undirected, weighted/unweighted, cyclic/acyclic.

all.question: What is adjacency matrix?
all.explanation: Adj matrix: O(V^2) space; O(1) edge query; dense graphs.

all.question: What is adjacency list?
options: Array of neighbor lists: graph[u] = [v1, v2, ...]
all.explanation: Adj list: O(V+E) space; O(degree(v)) edge query; sparse graphs.

all.question: What is an edge list?
all.explanation: Edge list: simple, O(E) space; best for Kruskal's MST algorithm.

all.question: What is BFS?
options: Breadth-First Search: explore level by level (queue)
all.explanation: BFS: shortest path in unweighted graph; O(V+E).

all.question: What is DFS?
all.explanation: DFS: topological sort, cycle detection, connected components; O(V+E).

all.question: What is topological sort?
all.explanation: Topological: Kahn's algorithm (in-degree) or DFS-based.

all.question: What is a DAG?
all.explanation: DAG: used for scheduling, dependency resolution, data flow.

all.question: What is a spanning tree?
options: Subgraph connecting all vertices with V-1 edges (no cycles)
all.explanation: Spanning tree: any tree connecting all vertices in graph.

all.question: What is a skip list?
options: Multi-level linked list for O(log n) search
all.explanation: Skip list: layers of linked lists; mimics binary search; simpler than balanced tree.

all.question: What is a Bloom filter?
options: Probabilistic set: no false negatives; may false positive
all.explanation: Bloom filter: bit array + k hash functions; insert sets bits; query checks bits.

all.question: What is a segment tree?
options: Tree for range queries and point updates (O(log n))
all.explanation: Segment tree: array representation; stores range attributes (sum, min, max).

all.question: What is a Fenwick tree?
options: Binary Indexed Tree: prefix sum queries O(log n)
all.explanation: Fenwick: simpler than segment tree; supports prefix sum and point update.

all.question: What is a disjoint set (Union-Find)?
all.explanation: Union-Find: find with path compression; union by rank; near O(1).

all.question: What is a suffix tree?
options: Compressed trie of all string suffixes
all.explanation: Suffix tree: O(m) substring search; pattern matching, LCS.

all.question: What is a suffix array?
options: Sorted array of all string suffixes
all.explanation: Suffix array: sorted starting positions; built O(n log n) or O(n).

all.question: What is LCP array?
options: Longest Common Prefix between adjacent suffixes
all.explanation: LCP with suffix array enables fast string search and LCS.

all.question: What is a sparse table?
options: Range query data structure: O(1) query, O(n log n) build
all.explanation: Sparse table: for idempotent ops (min, max, gcd); no updates.

all.question: What is a Cartesian tree?
options: Heap-ordered tree from array; root is minimum element
all.explanation: Cartesian tree: inorder = array order; heap property on values.

all.question: What is an interval tree?
all.explanation: Interval tree: center point; left/right subtrees for non-overlapping intervals.

all.question: What is a Range Tree?
all.explanation: Range tree: nested trees for k-d range searching.

all.question: What is a k-d tree?
all.explanation: k-d tree: each level splits on one dimension; nearest neighbor search.

all.question: What is a Quad Tree?
all.explanation: Quad tree: each node has 4 children (NE, NW, SE, SW); image compression.

all.question: What is an Octree?
options: 3D variant of Quadtree: 8 children per node
all.explanation: Octree: 3D space subdivision; used in 3D graphics, collision detection.

all.question: What is an R-tree?
options: Tree for spatial data (bounding boxes, GIS)
all.explanation: R-tree: each node = minimum bounding rectangle; GIS, spatial databases.

all.question: What is adjacency matrix vs list trade-off?
options: Matrix: O(V^2) space, O(1) edge check; List: O(V+E) space, O(deg) edge check
all.explanation: Choose based on graph density and edge query frequency.

all.question: What is amortized analysis?
options: Average cost per operation over sequence of operations
all.explanation: Amortized: dynamic array insertions cost O(1) amortized despite O(n) resize.

all.question: What is an invariant?
options: Property that always holds for data structure
all.explanation: BST invariant: left < parent < right. Heap invariant: parent >= children.

all.question: What is a circular buffer?
all.explanation: Circular buffer: read/write indices modulo size; producer-consumer.

all.question: What is an XOR linked list?
options: Memory-efficient doubly linked: stores XOR of prev/next
all.explanation: XOR list: ptr = prev XOR next; traverse with previous pointer.

all.question: What is a self-organizing list?
options: List reordered on access: move-to-front heuristic
all.explanation: Move-to-front: frequently accessed items near head; improves cache.

all.question: What is an unrolled linked list?
options: Linked list with array per node (reduces pointer overhead)
all.explanation: Unrolled: each node stores block of elements; cache-friendly.

all.question: What is a heap-ordered tree?
all.explanation: Heap-ordered: root is minimum; used in priority queues.

all.question: What is a pairing heap?
options: Simple heap: multi-way tree with lazy merge
all.explanation: Pairing heap: merge O(1); decrease-key O(log n) amortized.

all.question: What is a Fibonacci heap?
options: Advanced heap: O(1) insert/merge, O(log n) extract-min
all.explanation: Fibonacci: lazy consolidation; used in Dijkstra (V log V + E).

all.question: What is a binomial heap?
options: Collection of binomial trees; O(log n) operations
all.explanation: Binomial heap: each tree shape unique; efficient merge.

all.question: What is a hash trie?
options: Persistent hash table using array mapped trie
all.explanation: Hash trie: immutable, structure sharing; used in functional languages.

all.question: What is a rope?
options: Binary tree for string: efficient concat/split/insert
all.explanation: Rope: large text manipulation; O(log n) substring; used in text editors.

all.question: What is a gap buffer?
options: Array with gap for efficient insertion near cursor
all.explanation: Gap buffer: gap at cursor; typing fills gap; moves gap on cursor move.

all.question: What is a piece table?
options: Linked list of pieces describing original + added text
all.explanation: Piece table: undo/redo efficient; used in modern editors (VS Code).

all.question: What is a Z-order curve?
all.explanation: Z-order: interleave bits; preserves locality; used in DB indexing.

all.question: What is a Hilbert curve?
options: Space-filling curve with better locality than Z-order
all.explanation: Hilbert: visits each cell once; better cache performance in 2D.

all.question: What is a merkle tree?
options: Hash tree: leaf hashes, interior = hash(children)
all.explanation: Merkle tree: efficient data integrity verification; used in Git, blockchains.

all.question: What is a Patricia trie?
all.explanation: Patricia: memory efficient; O(m) search; used in IP routing.

all.question: What is a ternary search tree?
options: BST with 3 children (less, equal, greater) for strings
all.explanation: TST: combines BST and trie; space efficient for string dictionaries.

all.question: What is a van Emde Boas tree?
options: O(log log n) priority queue for integer universe
all.explanation: vEB tree: O(log log M) where M is universe size; uses recursive structure.

all.question: What is a link-cut tree?
options: Dynamic forest connectivity: cut, link, path queries
all.explanation: Link-cut: represents rooted forests; O(log n) operations.

all.question: What is a heavy-light decomposition?
options: Decomposes tree into heavy/light paths for path queries
all.explanation: HLD: heavy path = edge to heaviest child; allows path queries via segment tree.

all.question: What is monotonic stack?
options: Stack keeping elements in increasing/decreasing order
all.explanation: Monotonic stack: next greater element, histogram max area.

all.question: What is a sliding window?
all.explanation: Sliding window: O(n) for subarray problems (max, sum, unique).

all.question: What is the Two Pointer technique?
all.explanation: Two pointers: reverse array, two-sum, palindrome check.

all.question: What is a persistent data structure?
options: Immutable: previous versions preserved on modification
all.explanation: Persistent: structure sharing; copy only changed parts.

all.question: What is a concurrent data structure?
options: Thread-safe: multiple threads can access safely
all.explanation: Concurrent: lock-free, wait-free; ConcurrentHashMap, ConcurrentLinkedQueue.

all.question: What is a lock-free queue?
all.explanation: Lock-free: Michael-Scott queue; CAS on head/tail; no thread blocking.

all.question: What is a data structure?
options: Way to organize and store data for efficient access
all.explanation: Data structures: arrays, linked lists, trees, graphs, hash tables, stacks, queues.

all.question: What is an array?
options: Contiguous memory, fixed size, O(1) index access
all.explanation: Arrays store elements of same type at adjacent memory locations.

all.question: What is a dynamic array?
all.explanation: Dynamic array doubles capacity when full; amortized O(1) append.

all.question: What is a linked list?
options: Nodes with data and pointer to next node
all.explanation: Linked list: O(1) insert/delete at known position; O(n) access.

all.question: What is singly linked list?
options: Each node points to next only; forward traversal
all.explanation: Singly linked: head node; last node points to null.

all.question: What is doubly linked list?
options: Nodes point to both next and previous
all.explanation: Doubly linked: traversal both directions; prev pointer extra memory.

all.question: What is circular linked list?
options: Last node points to first node (loop)
all.explanation: Circular: useful for round-robin scheduling; no null end.

all.question: What is a stack?
options: LIFO: Last In, First Out data structure
all.explanation: Stack: push/pop O(1); used in function calls, expression evaluation, undo.

all.question: What is an algorithm?
options: Step-by-step procedure to solve a problem
all.explanation: Algorithms have inputs, outputs, definiteness, finiteness, effectiveness.

all.question: What is time complexity?
options: Measure of algorithm runtime vs input size
all.explanation: Time complexity: Big O notation describes growth rate.

all.question: What is space complexity?
options: Memory usage of algorithm vs input size
all.explanation: Space: O(1) in-place, O(n) auxiliary, O(n^2) matrix.

all.question: What is Big O notation?
all.explanation: Big O: f(n) = O(g(n)) if f(n) <= c * g(n) for n >= n0.

all.question: What is Theta notation?
options: Tight bound: both upper and lower
all.explanation: Theta: f(n) = Θ(g(n)) if c1*g(n) <= f(n) <= c2*g(n) for n >= n0.

all.question: What is Omega notation?
all.explanation: Omega: f(n) = Ω(g(n)) if f(n) >= c * g(n) for n >= n0.

all.question: What is O(1)?
options: Constant time: independent of input size
all.explanation: O(1): array access, hash table lookup, stack push/pop.

all.question: What is O(log n)?
all.explanation: O(log n): binary search, balanced tree operations.

all.question: What is O(n)?
all.explanation: O(n): linear search, finding max, array traversal.

all.question: What is O(n log n)?
all.explanation: O(n log n): merge sort, heap sort, quick sort average.

all.question: What is O(n^2)?
all.explanation: O(n^2): bubble sort, insertion sort, naive matrix multiply.

all.question: What is O(2^n)?
options: Exponential: doubles with each input increase
all.explanation: O(2^n): recursive Fibonacci (naive), subset generation.

all.question: What is O(n!)?
all.explanation: O(n!): traveling salesman brute force, permutation generation.

all.question: What is recursion?
options: Function calling itself with smaller input
all.explanation: Recursion: base case + recursive case; uses call stack.

all.question: What is tail recursion?
options: Recursive call is last operation (compiler can optimize)
all.explanation: Tail recursion: no pending computation after call; reusable stack frame.

all.question: What is divide and conquer?
all.explanation: D&C: Merge Sort (divide, sort, merge); Quick Sort (partition, sort).

all.question: What is dynamic programming?
all.explanation: DP: memoization (top-down) or tabulation (bottom-up).

all.question: What is memoization?
options: Caching function results for repeated inputs
all.explanation: Memoization: store expensive computations; typical in recursive DP.

all.question: What is tabulation?
all.explanation: Tabulation: fill array from base case to target; no recursion overhead.

all.question: What is optimal substructure?
all.explanation: Optimal substructure: shortest path, knapsack, edit distance.

all.question: What is overlapping subproblems?
all.explanation: Overlapping: Fibonacci(5) calls Fibonacci(4) and Fibonacci(3); memoize.

all.question: What is greedy algorithm?
options: Locally optimal choice at each step
all.explanation: Greedy: Huffman coding, Dijkstra, Prim, Kruskal; not always optimal.

all.question: What is the greedy choice property?
options: Locally optimal choice leads to global optimum
all.explanation: Greedy choice: coin change with canonical coins, activity selection.

all.question: What is backtracking?
options: Try solution, undo if fails (search space)
all.explanation: Backtracking: N-Queens, Sudoku, subset sum, graph coloring.

all.question: What is branch and bound?
all.explanation: B&B: reduces search space; used in TSP, integer programming.

all.question: What is linear search?
all.explanation: Linear: check each element; works on unsorted data.

all.question: What is binary search?
all.explanation: Binary: mid = (low + high) / 2; recurse left/right based on comparison.

all.question: What is jump search?
options: Jump sqrt(n) steps, then linear search; O(sqrt(n))
all.explanation: Jump: block size = sqrt(n); reduces comparisons vs linear.

all.question: What is interpolation search?
options: Binary search variant using value distribution; O(log log n) avg
all.explanation: Interpolation: probes based on key value; best for uniform distribution.

all.question: What is exponential search?
options: Exponential range + binary search; O(log i)
all.explanation: Exponential: find range [2^(k-1), 2^k]; binary search within.

all.question: What is sorting?
all.explanation: Sorting: comparison-based (comparison sort) or distribution-based.

all.question: What is stable sort?
options: Preserves relative order of equal elements
all.explanation: Stable: merge sort, insertion sort, timsort. Unstable: quick sort, heap sort.

all.question: What is in-place sort?
options: Uses O(1) or O(log n) extra space
all.explanation: In-place: bubble sort, heap sort, quick sort. Not in-place: merge sort.

all.question: What is bubble sort?
options: Repeatedly swap adjacent if out of order; O(n^2)
all.explanation: Bubble: after each pass, largest element bubbles to end. Optimized: stop if no swap.

all.question: What is selection sort?
options: Find min, swap with first; O(n^2)
all.explanation: Selection: O(n^2) always; not stable; minimum memory writes.

all.question: What is insertion sort?
options: Insert element into sorted portion; O(n^2) worst, O(n) best
all.explanation: Insertion: efficient for small or nearly sorted arrays; stable.

all.question: What is merge sort?
options: Divide, sort recursively, merge; O(n log n)
all.explanation: Merge: O(n log n) all cases; stable; O(n) extra space.

all.question: What is quick sort?
options: Select pivot, partition, recurse; O(n log n) avg, O(n^2) worst
all.explanation: Quick: in-place; worst when pivot is min/max; random pivot mitigates.

all.question: What is heap sort?
options: Build max-heap, extract repeatedly; O(n log n)
all.explanation: Heap: in-place, unstable; O(n) build, O(log n) per extract.

all.question: What is counting sort?
all.explanation: Counting sort: integer keys in range [0, k]; stable.

all.question: What is radix sort?
options: Sort digit by digit (LSD or MSD)
all.explanation: Radix: O(d * (n + k)) where d = digits; faster for fixed-length ints/strings.

all.question: What is bucket sort?
options: Distribute into buckets, sort each, concatenate
all.explanation: Bucket: O(n) avg for uniform distribution; O(n^2) worst.

all.question: What is timsort?
options: Adaptive merge sort + insertion sort (Python, Java)
all.explanation: Timsort: finds runs (increasing/decreasing); merges runs; O(n log n).

all.question: What is intro sort?
options: Hybrid: quick sort -> heap sort if recursion deep
all.explanation: Intro sort: begin with quick sort; if depth > 2*log n, switch to heap sort.

all.question: What is shell sort?
all.explanation: Shell: gap sequence; O(n log^2 n) worst; in-place, unstable.

all.question: What is comb sort?
all.explanation: Comb: gap shrink factor 1.3; eliminates turtles (small at end).

all.question: What is cycle sort?
all.explanation: Cycle: rotate cycles; useful when writing is expensive (flash memory).

all.question: What is pancake sort?
all.explanation: Pancake: flip largest unsorted to front, then flip to correct position.

all.question: What is bogo sort?
all.explanation: Bogo: check if sorted; if not, random shuffle. Bad.

all.question: What is Dijkstra's algorithm?
all.explanation: Dijkstra: greedy; use priority queue; O((V+E) log V).

all.question: What is Bellman-Ford?
options: Shortest path allowing negative edges; detects negative cycles
all.explanation: Bellman-Ford: relax all edges V-1 times; O(VE).

all.question: What is Floyd-Warshall?
all.explanation: Floyd-Warshall: DP[k][i][j] = min distance i->j via nodes 0..k.

all.question: What is Kruskal's algorithm?
options: MST: sort edges, add if no cycle (union-find)
all.explanation: Kruskal: O(E log E) sorting; works on disconnected graph.

all.question: What is Prim's algorithm?
options: MST: grow from node, add cheapest crossing edge
all.explanation: Prim: O(E log V) with heap; for dense graphs.

all.question: What is Boruvka's algorithm?
options: MST: each vertex connects cheapest edge; O(E log V)
all.explanation: Boruvka: works in phases; each vertex adds cheapest incident edge.

all.question: What is A* search?
options: Heuristic shortest path: f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
all.explanation: A*: admissible heuristic never overestimates; finds optimal path.

all.question: What is Huffman coding?
all.explanation: Huffman: build tree of character frequencies; assign shorter codes to frequent chars.

all.question: What is Knapsack (0/1)?
options: DP: maximize value within weight capacity
all.explanation: 0/1 Knapsack: DP[n][W] = max(include, exclude); O(nW).

all.question: What is fractional knapsack?
options: Greedy: take fraction of items; O(n log n)
all.explanation: Fractional: sort by value/weight; take until full.

all.question: What is LCS?
all.explanation: LCS: DP[i][j] = match or max(DP[i-1][j], DP[i][j-1]).

all.question: What is edit distance?
options: Minimum ops (insert, delete, replace) to transform string
all.explanation: Edit distance: DP[m][n]; O(mn). Operations have costs.

all.question: What is LIS?
options: Longest Increasing Subsequence: O(n log n) with patience sorting
all.explanation: LIS: DP O(n^2) or patience sorting with binary search O(n log n).

all.question: What is LPS?
all.explanation: LPS: DP[i][j] for i..j; if s[i]==s[j] DP[i+1][j-1] + 2 else max(DP[i+1][j], DP[i][j-1]).

all.question: What is matrix chain multiplication?
all.explanation: MCM: DP[i][j] = min splits; dimensions p[i-1] x p[i].

all.question: What is the coin change problem?
all.explanation: Coin change: DP[i] = min(DP[i - coin] + 1) for each coin.

all.question: What is the subset sum problem?
options: Check if subset sums to target; DP O(n*sum)
all.explanation: Subset sum: DP[i][s] = DP[i-1][s] OR DP[i-1][s - arr[i]].

all.question: What is the partition problem?
options: Can array be split into two equal sum subsets?
all.explanation: Partition: subset sum with target = total/2.

all.question: What is Kadane's algorithm?
all.explanation: Kadane: maxEndingHere = max(arr[i], maxEndingHere + arr[i]); maxSoFar = max(maxSoFar, maxEndingHere).

all.question: What is the two-sum problem?
options: Find pair with given sum; O(n) hash table
all.explanation: Two-sum: map complement = target - arr[i]; check if in hash set.

all.question: What is N-Queens?
options: Place N queens on NxN board without attack; backtracking
all.explanation: N-Queens: backtrack with column, diagonal, anti-diagonal constraints.

all.question: What is the Knight's tour?
options: Knight visits every square exactly once; backtracking
all.explanation: Knight: Warnsdorff's heuristic: move to square with fewest onward moves.

all.question: What is the Sudoku solver?
all.explanation: Sudoku: backtrack with row/col/box validation.

all.question: What is the traveling salesman problem?
options: Shortest route visiting all cities; NP-hard
all.explanation: TSP: brute force O(n!), DP Held-Karp O(n^2 * 2^n).

all.question: What is the vertex cover problem?
options: Minimum set of vertices covering all edges; NP-hard
all.explanation: Vertex cover: approximation (2-approx by picking both endpoints of edge).

all.question: What is the set cover problem?
options: Minimum number of sets covering all elements; NP-hard
all.explanation: Set cover: greedy approximation O(log n) of optimal.

all.question: What is the clique problem?
options: Find complete subgraph of size k; NP-complete
all.explanation: Clique: complementary to independent set in complement graph.

all.question: What is graph coloring?
options: Assign colors to vertices; adjacent different; NP-hard for >2
all.explanation: Graph coloring: greedy algorithm uses at most d+1 colors (d = max degree).

all.question: What is Hungarian algorithm?
options: Assignment problem: min cost matching; O(n^3)
all.explanation: Hungarian: matrix operations to find optimal assignment.

all.question: What is Gale-Shapley algorithm?
options: Stable matching: each man proposes; O(n^2)
all.explanation: Gale-Shapley: produce stable pairs; men optimal or women optimal.

all.question: What is max flow (Ford-Fulkerson)?
options: Max flow in network; O(E * max_flow)
all.explanation: Ford-Fulkerson: find augmenting path (DFS), increase flow until none.

all.question: What is Edmonds-Karp?
all.explanation: Edmonds-Karp: BFS finds shortest augmenting path; polynomial time.

all.question: What is Dinic's algorithm?
options: Max flow with level graph + blocking flow; O(V^2 E)
all.explanation: Dinic: faster than Edmonds-Karp for dense/unit graphs.

all.question: What is min cut?
options: Minimum capacity separating source from sink; = max flow
all.explanation: Min cut: set of edges; removed disconnects source from sink.

all.question: What is bipartite matching?
options: Maximum matching in bipartite graph; O(VE) via augmenting paths
all.explanation: Bipartite matching: Hopcroft-Karp O(E sqrt(V)).

all.question: What is Strassen's algorithm?
options: Matrix multiplication: O(n^2.807) vs O(n^3) naive
all.explanation: Strassen: 7 multiplications instead of 8 for 2x2; recursive divide.

all.question: What is the Fast Fourier Transform?
all.explanation: FFT: Cooley-Tukey; divide even/odd coefficients; butterfly operations.

all.question: What is Karatsuba multiplication?
all.explanation: Karatsuba: split numbers; 3 multiplications instead of 4.

all.question: What is the Euclidean algorithm?
options: GCD by repeated division: O(log min(a,b))
all.explanation: gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b); base when b = 0 returns a.

all.question: What is the extended Euclidean algorithm?
options: Finds x,y such that ax + by = gcd(a,b)
all.explanation: Extended: back-substitute through Euclidean steps to find coefficients.

all.question: What is modular exponentiation?
options: Compute a^b mod m efficiently; O(log b)
all.explanation: Exponentiation by squaring: a^b = (a^(b/2))^2 if b even, a * a^(b-1) if odd.

all.question: What is Sieve of Eratosthenes?
options: Find all primes up to n; O(n log log n)
all.explanation: Sieve: mark multiples of each prime starting from 2.

all.question: What is primality test?
options: Check if number is prime; trial division O(sqrt(n))
all.explanation: Miller-Rabin: probabilistic O(log n); deterministic for n < 3.3e24.

all.question: What is Rabin-Karp?
options: Substring search with rolling hash; O(n+m) avg
all.explanation: Rabin-Karp: hash(pattern); sliding window hash on text; verify on match.

all.question: What is KMP?
all.explanation: KMP: LPS prefix function avoids redundant comparisons.

all.question: What is Boyer-Moore?
options: Substring search with bad character + good suffix heuristics
all.explanation: Boyer-Moore: skips characters using precomputed shift tables; sub-linear avg.

all.question: What is the Z algorithm?
options: Compute Z-array (longest prefix match at each position)
all.explanation: Z-array: Z[i] = longest common prefix of s and s[i:]; O(n).

all.question: What is Manacher's algorithm?
all.explanation: Manacher: maintain center/rightmost; expand mirrors; O(n).

all.question: What is an algorithm?
options: Step-by-step procedure to solve a problem
all.explanation: Algorithms have inputs, outputs, definiteness, finiteness, effectiveness.

all.question: What is time complexity?
options: Measure of algorithm runtime vs input size
all.explanation: Time complexity: Big O notation describes growth rate.

all.question: What is space complexity?
options: Memory usage of algorithm vs input size
all.explanation: Space: O(1) in-place, O(n) auxiliary, O(n^2) matrix.

all.question: What is SDLC?
options: Software Development Life Cycle: plan, design, code, test, deploy, maintain
all.explanation: SDLC phases: Requirements, Design, Implementation, Testing, Deployment, Maintenance.

all.question: What is Waterfall?
options: Sequential SDLC: each phase completes before next
all.explanation: Waterfall: Requirements -> Design -> Code -> Test -> Deploy. Rigid, no backward flow.

all.question: What is Agile?
all.explanation: Agile: sprints, daily standups, retrospectives; adaptive to change.

all.question: What is Scrum?
options: Agile framework with fixed-length sprints and roles
all.explanation: Scrum: Product Owner, Scrum Master, Dev Team; Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Review, Retro.

all.question: What is Sprint?
all.explanation: Sprint delivers potentially shippable product increment.

all.question: What is Product Backlog?
options: Prioritized list of features, bugs, tech tasks
all.explanation: Product Backlog: user stories ordered by value; refined continuously.

all.question: What is Sprint Backlog?
options: Selected backlog items for current sprint
all.explanation: Sprint Backlog: committed work; visible on task board (To Do, In Progress, Done).

all.question: What is user story?
options: Feature from user perspective: "As a [user], I want [goal]"
all.explanation: User story: As a user, I want to reset my password so I can regain access.

all.question: What is acceptance criteria?
options: Conditions that story must satisfy for closure
all.explanation: Given/When/Then format: Given logged in, When I click reset, Then email is sent.

all.question: What is Definition of Done?
options: Team's shared understanding of when work is complete
all.explanation: DoD: code reviewed, tested, documented, deployed to staging.

all.question: What is estimation?
options: Relative sizing: story points, t-shirt sizes, ideal hours
all.explanation: Estimation uses Fibonacci-like sequence: 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21.

all.question: What is velocity?
options: Sum of story points completed per sprint
all.explanation: Velocity measures team throughput; used for sprint planning predictability.

all.question: What is burndown chart?
options: Shows remaining work vs time in sprint
all.explanation: Burndown: ideal line (straight) vs actual line; tracks progress.

all.question: What is burndown vs burnup?
options: Burndown: remaining work; Burnup: completed work
all.explanation: Burnup shows total work and completed work separately.

all.question: What is daily standup?
options: Daily 15-min meeting: what I did, what I'll do, blockers
all.explanation: Standup keeps team aligned; not status report but planning sync.

all.question: What is Sprint Review?
all.explanation: Review: demo working software; gather feedback for backlog.

all.question: What is Sprint Retrospective?
options: Reflect on process: what went well, what to improve
all.explanation: Retro: team discusses improvements for next sprint (action items).

all.question: What is Kanban?
all.explanation: Kanban: limit WIP, manage flow, no fixed iterations; pull-based.

all.question: What is WIP limit?
options: Work In Progress limit prevents overloading team
all.explanation: WIP limit: max 3 items per column; reduces context switching.

all.question: What is Lean?
all.explanation: Lean: eliminate waste, amplify learning, defer commitment, deliver fast.

all.question: What is XP (Extreme Programming)?
options: Agile with engineering practices: TDD, pair programming
all.explanation: XP: frequent releases, customer on-site, coding standards, collective ownership.

all.question: What is TDD?
options: Test-Driven Development: write test first, then code
all.explanation: TDD: Red (write failing test) -> Green (make pass) -> Refactor.

all.question: What is pair programming?
options: Two devs at one workstation: driver + navigator
all.explanation: Pair programming improves code quality; knowledge sharing; fewer bugs.

all.question: What is mob programming?
options: Entire team at one workstation solves problem
all.explanation: Mob: one driver, rest navigators; rotates driver frequently.

all.question: What is code review?
options: Systematic peer examination of code changes
all.explanation: Code review: pull request review; find defects, improve design, share knowledge.

all.question: What is continuous integration?
options: Frequent merging with automated build + tests
all.explanation: CI: every push triggers build + test suite; fails fast on errors.

all.question: What is continuous delivery?
options: Automated deployment to production after CI passes
all.explanation: CD: deploy to staging, then production with manual approval gate.

all.question: What is continuous deployment?
options: Every successful pass deploys automatically to production
all.explanation: CD: fully automated; every merge to main goes live immediately.

all.question: What is DevOps?
options: Combines development and operations; automation culture
all.explanation: DevOps: CI/CD, infrastructure as code, monitoring, collaboration.

all.question: What is a build tool?
options: Automates compilation, testing, packaging: Maven, Gradle, Webpack
all.explanation: Build tools manage dependencies and automate repetitive build tasks.

all.question: What is version control?
options: Tracks changes to code over time: Git, SVN, Mercurial
all.explanation: Git: distributed version control; commits, branches, merges, tags.

all.question: What is Git?
all.explanation: Git: local and remote repos; branching/merging; staging area.

all.question: What is branching?
options: Divergent development: git branch feature-x creates branch
all.explanation: Git branching enables parallel development; merge back via pull request.

all.question: What is merge vs rebase?
options: Merge: combine branches with history; Rebase: linearize history
all.explanation: Merge creates merge commit; rebase rewrites commit history (cleaner).

all.question: What is pull request?
options: Proposed code change with review process
all.explanation: PR: propose changes, discuss, review, then merge into target branch.

all.question: What is a commit?
options: Snapshot of changes with message: git commit -m "fix bug"
all.explanation: Commit: atomic change set; referenced by SHA hash.

all.question: What is a tag?
options: Named reference to specific commit (usually version)
all.explanation: git tag v1.0.0 marks release version; lightweight or annotated.

all.question: What is a release?
all.explanation: Release: semantic versioning (major.minor.patch); release notes.

all.question: What is semantic versioning?
options: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH: incompatible API, new feature, bug fix
all.explanation: SemVer: 2.0.0 breaking change, 2.1.0 new feature, 2.1.1 bug fix.

all.question: What is refactoring?
all.explanation: Refactoring improves maintainability: extract method, rename, move class.

all.question: What is technical debt?
options: Cost of quick-and-dirty solutions needing later rework
all.explanation: Technical debt accumulates from shortcuts; pay down via refactoring.

all.question: What is unit testing?
all.explanation: Unit test: @Test void add_shouldReturnSum() { assertEquals(5, calc.add(2, 3)); }

all.question: What is integration testing?
all.explanation: Integration test: test API endpoint calls database and returns correct response.

all.question: What is system testing?
all.explanation: System test: simulate user workflows across all layers.

all.question: What is acceptance testing?
all.explanation: UAT: customer or QA validates features against acceptance criteria.

all.question: What is regression testing?
options: Re-running tests to ensure no new bugs from changes
all.explanation: Regression: automated suite runs before every release.

all.question: What is smoke test?
options: Quick sanity check of critical functionality
all.explanation: Smoke: verify basic flow (login, create, read) after deploy.

all.question: What is mock?
options: Fake object simulating real dependency for testing
all.explanation: Mock: verify interactions: verify(mock).send(email); assert called.

all.question: What is stub?
options: Provides canned answers to calls during test
all.explanation: Stub: when(storage.fetch(1)).thenReturn(new User("John")).

all.question: What is test coverage?
options: Percentage of code exercised by tests
all.explanation: Coverage: line, branch, path; 80%+ is typical target.

all.question: What is mutation testing?
options: Introduces bugs to test if tests catch them
all.explanation: Mutation testing (PIT): modifies code; tests should fail.

all.question: What is fuzz testing?
options: Random inputs to find crashes/security issues
all.explanation: Fuzzing: automated random input generation; finds edge cases.

all.question: What is performance testing?
options: Tests speed, responsiveness, stability under load
all.explanation: Performance: JMeter, Gatling, k6 simulate concurrent users.

all.question: What is load testing?
all.explanation: Load: simulate 1000 concurrent users browsing site.

all.question: What is stress testing?
options: System behavior beyond normal capacity (breaking point)
all.explanation: Stress: gradual increase until system fails; find breaking point.

all.question: What is usability testing?
options: Real users interact with interface; observe issues
all.explanation: Usability: observe users completing tasks; identify UI friction points.

all.question: What is A/B testing?
options: Compare two versions to determine which performs better
all.explanation: A/B: serve variant A to 50%, B to 50%; compare conversion metrics.

all.question: What is requirement?
options: Condition or capability needed by user
all.explanation: Functional: what system does; Non-functional: performance, security, scale.

all.question: What is functional requirement?
options: Specifies what system should do (behaviors, features)
all.explanation: Functional: user can log in, search products, place order.

all.question: What is non-functional requirement?
options: Quality attributes: performance, security, usability, scalability
all.explanation: Non-functional: response time < 2s, 99.9% uptime, OAuth2 auth.

all.question: What is use case?
options: Description of actor interacting with system for goal
all.explanation: Use case: Actor: Customer; Goal: Withdraw cash; Steps: insert card, enter PIN, select amount.

all.question: What is UML?
options: Unified Modeling Language: diagrams for system design
all.explanation: UML: class, sequence, use case, activity, state, component diagrams.

all.question: What is class diagram?
all.explanation: Class diagram: inheritance (arrow), composition (filled diamond), aggregation (empty diamond).

all.question: What is sequence diagram?
options: UML: interactions over time, lifelines, messages
all.explanation: Sequence: actor -> controller -> service -> database; shows method calls.

all.question: What is activity diagram?
options: UML: workflow from start to end (actions, decisions)
all.explanation: Activity: start -> action -> decision [yes/no] -> end; parallel forks.

all.question: What is state diagram?
options: UML: states and transitions of an object
all.explanation: State diagram: Order states: New -> Paid -> Shipped -> Delivered.

all.question: What is component diagram?
options: UML: high-level system components and dependencies
all.explanation: Component: WebApp -> API -> Database; shows interfaces and ports.

all.question: What is deployment diagram?
options: UML: physical deployment of artifacts on nodes
all.explanation: Deployment: application server, database server, load balancer nodes.

all.question: What is a design document?
options: Technical spec describing architecture and design decisions
all.explanation: Design doc: overview, architecture, data model, APIs, trade-offs.

all.question: What is an API contract?
options: Agreed interface between client and server
all.explanation: API contract: OpenAPI/Swagger spec; defines endpoints, params, responses.

all.question: What is a wireframe?
options: Low-fidelity visual guide for UI layout
all.explanation: Wireframe: boxes and text for layout; no colors or images.

all.question: What is a mockup?
all.explanation: Mockup: shows actual colors, typography, spacing; not interactive.

all.question: What is a prototype?
all.explanation: Prototype: clickable UI; user can navigate and test flows.

all.question: What is a milestone?
all.explanation: Milestone: alpha release, beta, feature freeze, GA.

all.question: What is a risk?
all.explanation: Risk: identify probability and impact; mitigation plan.

all.question: What is a dependency?
options: External factor team relies on (third-party, API, other team)
all.explanation: Dependency: waiting for payment gateway API; block release if delayed.

all.question: What is a blocker?
options: Issue preventing progress; needs immediate resolution
all.explanation: Blocker: production outage preventing testing; escalate for resolution.

all.question: What is RCA?
options: Root Cause Analysis: find underlying cause of problem
all.explanation: RCA: 5 Whys, fishbone diagram; prevent recurrence by addressing root cause.

all.question: What is postmortem?
options: Documented analysis of incident after resolution
all.explanation: Postmortem: what happened, why, how to prevent; blameless culture.

all.question: What is an SLA?
options: Service Level Agreement: uptime, response time guarantees
all.explanation: SLA: 99.9% uptime, critical bug response < 1 hour.

all.question: What is SLO?
options: Service Level Objective: internal target (more ambitious than SLA)
all.explanation: SLO: 99.95% uptime internal target vs 99.9% SLA commitment.

all.question: What is SLI?
options: Service Level Indicator: measured metric (latency, error rate)
all.explanation: SLI: actual measured uptime or request latency p99.

all.question: What is incident management?
all.explanation: Incident: detect -> respond -> resolve -> communicate -> postmortem.

all.question: What is monitoring?
options: Collecting metrics, logs, traces from systems
all.explanation: Monitoring: Prometheus, Grafana, DataDog; track CPU, memory, error rates.

all.question: What is logging?
options: Structured records of events: INFO, WARN, ERROR
all.explanation: Logging: centralized (ELK, Loki); correlation ID across services.

all.question: What is observability?
options: Understanding system state from outputs (logs, metrics, traces)
all.explanation: Observability: query, correlate, drill down without new code.

all.question: What is distributed tracing?
all.explanation: Tracing: Jaeger, Zipkin; trace ID propagated via headers; span per service.

all.question: What is a runbook?
options: Operations manual for common tasks and incidents
all.explanation: Runbook: step-by-step procedures for restart, scale, debug, rollback.

all.question: What is chaos engineering?
options: Proactively inject failures to test system resilience
all.explanation: Chaos: Netflix Chaos Monkey kills random instances; verify system survives.

all.question: What is blue-green deploy?
options: Two identical environments; switch traffic on deploy
all.explanation: Blue-green: deploy to green; switch router to green; rollback to blue.

all.question: What is canary release?
options: Gradual traffic shift to new version
all.explanation: Canary: 5% -> 20% -> 50% -> 100%; monitor for errors at each step.

all.question: What is feature toggle?
options: Flag to enable/disable feature without deploy
all.explanation: Feature toggle: LaunchDarkly; kill switch for quick rollback.

all.question: What is dark launch?
options: Deploy feature hidden behind toggle; enable later
all.explanation: Dark: code deployed but inactive; toggle on when ready.

all.question: What is database migration?
options: Schema changes applied versioned and automated
all.explanation: Migration: Flyway, Liquibase; up/down scripts for versioning.

all.question: What is rollback?
options: Revert to previous version (code, schema, data)
all.explanation: Rollback: git revert, database migration down, deploy previous artifact.

all.question: What is capacity planning?
options: Predicting resources needed for expected load
all.explanation: Capacity: estimate users, transactions, storage; scale accordingly.

all.question: What is scalability?
options: System ability to handle growing load
all.explanation: Scalability: horizontal (more servers) vs vertical (bigger server).

all.question: What is horizontal scaling?
options: Adding more servers to distribute load
all.explanation: Horizontal: add instances behind load balancer; shared-nothing design.

all.question: What is vertical scaling?
options: Upgrading existing server with more CPU/RAM
all.explanation: Vertical: bigger machine; simpler but has upper limit.

all.question: What is a microservice?
options: Independent, loosely coupled service with single responsibility
all.explanation: Microservice: bounded context, own data store, independent deploy.

all.question: What is computer organization?
all.explanation: Computer org: circuits, control signals, memory types, I/O mechanisms.

all.question: What is computer architecture?
options: Design of instruction set and system structure
all.explanation: Architecture: ISA, addressing modes, data types, registers visible to programmer.

all.question: What is the von Neumann architecture?
all.explanation: von Neumann: instructions and data share same memory space (bottleneck).

all.question: What is Harvard architecture?
options: Separate memory for instructions and data
all.explanation: Harvard: simultaneous instruction fetch and data access (pipelining benefit).

all.question: What is a bit?
options: Binary digit: 0 or 1, smallest data unit
all.explanation: 8 bits = 1 byte; 4 bits = 1 nibble.

all.question: What is a byte?
all.explanation: Byte = 8 bits; 1024 bytes = 1 KB.

all.question: What is a word?
options: Natural data unit for processor (16, 32, or 64 bits)
all.explanation: 32-bit processor: word = 32 bits; 64-bit: word = 64 bits.

all.question: What is octal number system?
all.explanation: Octal: 17 = 1*8 + 7 = 15 decimal.

all.question: What is hexadecimal?
all.explanation: Hex: 0xFF = 15*16 + 15 = 255 decimal.

all.question: What is 1's complement?
all.explanation: 1s complement: 5 (0101) -> -5 (1010); zero has two representations.

all.question: What is 2's complement?
options: Invert bits + 1; standard signed integer representation

all.question: What is sign extension?
options: Expanding smaller signed value to larger bit width
all.explanation: Sign extension copies sign bit: 8-bit -1 (11111111) -> 16-bit (1111111111111111).

all.question: What is a logic gate?
options: Basic digital circuit: AND, OR, NOT, NAND, NOR, XOR, XNOR
all.explanation: AND: both inputs 1 -> output 1; OR: at least one 1 -> output 1; NOT: inverts.

all.question: What is a truth table?
options: Table showing all input combinations and outputs

all.question: What is a half adder?
options: Adds 2 bits: sum = A XOR B, carry = A AND B
all.explanation: Half adder: 2 inputs, 2 outputs (sum, carry); no input carry.

all.question: What is a full adder?
options: Adds 3 bits (A, B, C_in): sum = A XOR B XOR C_in, carry = (A AND B) OR (C_in AND (A XOR B))
all.explanation: Full adder: 3 inputs, 2 outputs; used in multi-bit adders.

all.question: What is a ripple carry adder?
options: Full adders cascaded; carry propagates through each stage
all.explanation: Ripple carry: simple but slow; carry must ripple through all bits.

all.question: What is carry look-ahead?
options: Pre-computes carry using generate (G) and propagate (P)
all.explanation: CLA: G = A AND B; P = A XOR B; carry computed in parallel; faster.

all.question: What is an ALU?
options: Arithmetic Logic Unit: performs arithmetic and logic operations
all.explanation: ALU: ADD, SUB, AND, OR, XOR, NOT, shift; outputs result and flags.

all.question: What is a multiplexer (MUX)?
options: Selects one of multiple inputs based on select lines
all.explanation: MUX: 2-to-1 MUX has 2 inputs, 1 select, 1 output.

all.question: What is a demultiplexer (DEMUX)?
options: Routes single input to one of multiple outputs
all.explanation: DEMUX: 1-to-4 DEMUX has 1 input, 2 selects, 4 outputs.

all.question: What is a decoder?
options: Converts n-bit binary to 2^n unique outputs
all.explanation: Decoder: 2-to-4 decoder: input 00 -> output 0001, 01 -> 0010, etc.

all.question: What is an encoder?
options: Converts 2^n unique inputs to n-bit binary
all.explanation: Encoder: priority encoder: multiple inputs; highest priority wins.

all.question: What is a flip-flop?
all.explanation: D flip-flop: Q = D on clock edge; used in registers and counters.

all.question: What is SR latch?
all.explanation: SR latch: S=1,R=0 -> Q=1; S=0,R=1 -> Q=0; S=1,R=1 invalid.

all.question: What is JK flip-flop?
all.explanation: JK: J=1,K=0 -> set; J=0,K=1 -> reset; J=1,K=1 -> toggle.

all.question: What is D flip-flop?
options: Data flip-flop: Q follows D on clock edge
all.explanation: D flip-flop: D=0 -> Q=0; D=1 -> Q=1; simplest for storage.

all.question: What is a register?
options: Group of flip-flops storing multiple bits
all.explanation: Register: 8-bit register = 8 D flip-flops with shared clock.

all.question: What is a shift register?
options: Shifts bits left/right on each clock
all.explanation: Shift register: serial in parallel out, or parallel in serial out.

all.question: What is a counter?
all.explanation: Counter: 4-bit binary counter counts 0 to 15; can be up/down.

all.question: What is the control unit?
options: Directs operation of processor: fetch, decode, execute
all.explanation: Control unit: generates control signals (register write, ALU op, memory read/write).

all.question: What is hardwired control?
options: Control unit with combinational logic gates (fast, fixed)
all.explanation: Hardwired: complex for CISC; used in RISC for speed.

all.question: What is microprogrammed control?
options: Control signals stored in control memory (microinstructions)
all.explanation: Microprogrammed: flexible; microinstructions generate control signals step by step.

all.question: What is a microinstruction?
options: One step in microprogram: control signals for one cycle
all.explanation: Microinstruction: bits for register transfers, ALU op, memory access.

all.question: What is an instruction?
options: Command in machine language: opcode + operands
all.explanation: Instruction: ADD R1, R2, R3 (opcode ADD, registers R1, R2, R3).

all.question: What is instruction format?
options: Layout of bits in instruction: opcode, register, address fields
all.explanation: R-type: opcode, rs, rt, rd, shamt, funct; I-type: opcode, rs, rt, immediate.

all.question: What is addressing mode?
options: How operand address is computed: immediate, direct, indirect, indexed
all.explanation: Immediate: operand in instruction; Direct: address in instruction; Register: operand in register.

all.question: What is immediate addressing?
options: Operand value directly in instruction: ADD R1, #5
all.explanation: Immediate: no memory access; fast but limited to small constants.

all.question: What is direct addressing?
options: Address field contains operand memory address
all.explanation: Direct: LOAD R1, 1000 loads from memory address 1000.

all.question: What is register addressing?
options: Operand in register; register field holds register number
all.explanation: Register: ADD R1, R2, R3 (operands R2, R3; result to R1).

all.question: What is indirect addressing?
options: Register or memory location holds effective address
all.explanation: Indirect: LOAD R1, @1000 reads address from memory[1000], then loads from that address.

all.question: What is indexed addressing?
all.explanation: Indexed: LOAD R1, array(R2) where R2 is index; base is array label.

all.question: What is the instruction cycle?
options: Fetch -> Decode -> Execute -> Memory Access -> Write Back
all.explanation: Instruction cycle may take multiple clock cycles per stage.

all.question: What is the fetch stage?
options: Fetch instruction from memory using PC; increment PC
all.explanation: Fetch: send PC to memory; read instruction; PC = PC + 4.

all.question: What is the decode stage?
all.explanation: Decode: determine operation and operand sources from instruction bits.

all.question: What is the execute stage?
options: Perform ALU operation (ADD, SUB, AND, etc.)
all.explanation: Execute: ALU computes result based on opcode and operands.

all.question: What is pipelining?
options: Overlapping instruction execution: each stage works in parallel
all.explanation: Pipeline: 5-stage: IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB; increases throughput.

all.question: What are pipeline hazards?
options: Next instruction cannot execute in following cycle
all.explanation: Hazards: structural (resource conflict), data (dependency), control (branch).

all.question: What is a structural hazard?
options: Two instructions need same hardware resource
all.explanation: Structural: IF and MEM both need memory; resolved with separate I-cache/D-cache.

all.question: What is a data hazard?
options: Instruction depends on result of previous instruction
all.explanation: Data hazard: ADD R1, R2; SUB R3, R1 (SUB needs R1 from ADD).

all.question: What is a control hazard?
options: Branch changes PC; pipeline may fetch wrong path
all.explanation: Control hazard: branch outcome not known until EX stage; misprediction penalty.

all.question: What is forwarding (bypassing)?
options: Routes result from EX stage directly to dependent instruction
all.explanation: Forwarding: pass ALU result from EX to next instruction EX input; avoid stall.

all.question: What is branch prediction?
options: Predict branch outcome to avoid control hazard stalls
all.explanation: Branch prediction: simple (always taken), dynamic (history table).

all.question: What is speculative execution?
options: Executing predicted path before branch is resolved
all.explanation: Speculative: execute predicted path; flush on mispredict.

all.question: What is a memory hierarchy?
options: Registers -> Cache -> RAM -> Disk; speed/capacity trade-off
all.explanation: Memory hierarchy: faster, smaller, more expensive at top; slower, larger, cheaper below.

all.question: What is cache memory?
options: Small, fast SRAM between CPU and main memory
all.explanation: Cache: stores frequently accessed data; reduces average memory access time.

all.question: What is cache hit?
all.explanation: Cache hit: data served from cache quickly (few cycles).

all.question: What is cache miss?
options: Requested data not in cache; fetch from main memory
all.explanation: Cache miss penalty: block fetched from next level (10-100 cycles for DRAM).

all.question: What is cache line?
options: Fixed-size block of data transferred between cache and memory
all.explanation: Cache line: typically 64 bytes; aligned to memory block.

all.question: What is direct-mapped cache?
options: Each memory block maps to exactly one cache line
all.explanation: Direct-mapped: block address % number of lines; simple, conflict misses.

all.question: What is fully associative cache?
options: Any memory block can map to any cache line
all.explanation: Fully associative: searched all lines; most flexible, expensive hardware.

all.question: What is set-associative cache?
options: N-way: each set has N lines; block maps to set
all.explanation: 4-way set-associative: 4 lines per set; balances conflict/cost.

all.question: What is LRU replacement?
options: Evicts least recently used cache line
all.explanation: LRU: track access order; evict oldest used line.

all.question: What is write-through?
options: Write to cache AND main memory simultaneously
all.explanation: Write-through: slower writes but consistent; simpler.

all.question: What is write-back?
options: Write only to cache; write to memory on eviction (dirty bit)
all.explanation: Write-back: faster writes; memory updated when dirty line evicted.

all.question: What is virtual memory?
options: Uses disk as extension of RAM; page-based address translation
all.explanation: Virtual memory: TLB translates virtual to physical address; page fault goes to disk.

all.question: What is a page?
all.explanation: Page: contiguously in virtual memory; mapped to physical frame.

all.question: What is a page table?
options: Per-process mapping from virtual page -> physical frame
all.explanation: Page table: indexed by virtual page number; entry has frame number + flags.

all.question: What is TLB?
options: Translation Lookaside Buffer: cache for recent page table entries
all.explanation: TLB: fast, small; caches recent VPN->PFN mappings; miss goes to page table.

all.question: What is a page fault?
options: Accessing page not in physical memory (must load from disk)
all.explanation: Page fault: OS selects victim page, loads required page from disk.

all.question: What is thrashing?
options: Excessive page faults; system spends all time paging
all.explanation: Thrashing: too many active processes; each page faults constantly.

all.question: What is an interrupt?
options: Hardware signal pausing CPU to service device
all.explanation: Interrupt: device needs attention; CPU saves state, runs handler.

all.question: What is an exception?
options: Synchronous CPU event: divide by zero, page fault, syscall
all.explanation: Exception: caused by executing instruction; handler may fix or abort.

all.question: What is a trap?
all.explanation: Trap: syscall instruction switches to kernel mode for OS service.

all.question: What is a system call?
options: Request to OS kernel: open, read, write, fork, exit
all.explanation: Syscall: parameters in registers; trap to kernel; return value in register.

all.question: What is interrupt vector?
options: Table mapping interrupt number to handler address
all.explanation: Interrupt vector table: entry n contains address of handler for interrupt n.

all.question: What is DMA?
options: Direct Memory Access: device transfers data without CPU
all.explanation: DMA controller transfers blocks; CPU notified by interrupt on completion.

all.question: What is I/O mapping?
all.explanation: Memory-mapped: device registers in memory address space; port: in/out instructions.

all.question: What is polling?
options: CPU repeatedly checks device status register
all.explanation: Polling: simple but wastes CPU cycles if device is slow.

all.question: What is a bus?
all.explanation: System bus: data, address, control lines; connects CPU, memory, I/O.

all.question: What is synchronous bus?
all.explanation: Synchronous: all devices use same clock; simpler, limited by longest path.

all.question: What is asynchronous bus?
all.explanation: Asynchronous: devices negotiate timing; works with diverse speeds.

all.question: What is bus arbitration?
options: Determines which device controls bus next
all.explanation: Arbitration: daisy chain, centralized (arbiter), distributed.

all.question: What is RISC?
options: Reduced Instruction Set Computer: simple, single-cycle instructions
all.explanation: RISC: load/store architecture; fixed 32-bit instructions; many registers.

all.question: What is CISC?
options: Complex Instruction Set Computer: variable-length, complex instructions
all.explanation: CISC: x86; variable length; instruction can access memory directly.

all.question: What is Superscalar?
options: Multiple instructions per clock cycle (multiple execution units)
all.explanation: Superscalar: hardware checks dependencies; issues multiple instructions.

all.question: What is VLIW?
options: Very Long Instruction Word: compiler packs multiple operations
all.explanation: VLIW: compiler handles scheduling; simple hardware.

all.question: What is EPIC?
all.explanation: EPIC: compiler bundles instructions; predication; speculation support.

all.question: What is SIMD?
options: Single Instruction, Multiple Data: vector processing
all.explanation: SIMD: SSE/AVX; one instruction operates on multiple data elements.

all.question: What is MIMD?
options: Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data: multi-core processors
all.explanation: MIMD: each core fetches/executes own instruction stream on own data.

all.question: What is Flynn's taxonomy?
options: Classifies architectures: SISD, SIMD, MISD, MIMD
all.explanation: Flynn: based on instruction and data stream counts.

all.question: What is multi-core?
options: Multiple processor cores on single chip
all.explanation: Multi-core: 2-16 cores; shares cache; parallel processing.

all.question: What is hyper-threading?
options: One physical core appears as two logical cores
all.explanation: HT: share execution units; OS sees 2 logical CPUs per physical.

all.question: What is Amdahl's law?
options: Speedup limited by sequential portion: S <= 1 / (1 - P + P/N)
all.explanation: Amdahl: if 50% is parallel, max speedup is 2x (even with infinite cores).

all.question: What is Moore's law?
all.explanation: Moore: 2x transistors per area; slowing down since ~2010.

all.question: What is Dennard scaling?
options: Power density constant as transistors shrink
all.explanation: Dennard: smaller transistors -> same power/area; ended ~2006 (dark silicon).

all.question: What is a GPU?
options: Graphics Processing Unit: many cores for parallel work
all.explanation: GPU: thousands of simple cores; excels at SIMD workloads (matrix multiply).

all.question: What is RAS?
all.explanation: RAS: ECC memory, redundant components, hot swap, error recovery.

all.question: What is ECC memory?
options: Error-Correcting Code memory: detects and corrects single-bit errors
all.explanation: ECC: additional bits per word; SECDED (single error correct, double error detect).
